title: Mate-choice copying in non-human animals: an update of two meta-analyses
text:
Project Overview
This online supplement provides the complete computational workflow and interactive reports for an updated systematic review and multi-level meta-analysis of mate-choice copying (MCC) across animal taxa.
Mate-choice copying is a form of socially learned preference: an individual’s evaluation of a potential mate changes after it observes the mate choices of others. This synthesis aims to:
crumbs: 1  Mate-choice copying in non-human animals: an update of two meta-analyses.

title: Mate-choice copying in non-human animals: an update of two meta-analyses
text:
What this Supplement Contains
Each phase of the meta-analysis is documented so that it can be reproduced:

Literature screening: how records from the two databases were deduplicated, scored for completeness, randomly shuffled, and split among the screening team.
Data extraction: how the included papers were processed through an AI-assisted extraction pipeline (Google NotebookLM, with Google Gemini and Claude Code) and how the extracted numbers were verified.
Effect-size synthesis: how continuous outcomes (Hedges’ \(g\)) and binary outcomes (odds ratios) were calculated, combined in multi-level random-effects models, and visualised.
Publication-bias diagnostics: funnel plots, Egger’s regression tests, small-study-effect corrections, and tests for a decline in effect size over time.
crumbs: 1  Mate-choice copying in non-human animals: an update of two meta-analyses.

title: Mate-choice copying in non-human animals: an update of two meta-analyses
text:
How to Navigate
Use the floating left sidebar or the search bar above to browse the supplement’s chapters:

Home (index.qmd) — This overview page.
Literature Screening & Processing Pipeline (literature_screening.qmd) — The bibliographic search, deduplication, assignment of articles to screeners, AI-assisted data extraction, and the planned analyses.
Overall Effect Size Calculation & Synthesis (overall_effect.qmd) — Fits the multi-level models and reports pooled effects for three data subsets (new extraction, original datasets, and the two combined), with side-by-side orchard plots.
Publication Bias Diagnostics (publication_bias.qmd) — Inspects funnel-plot symmetry, runs Egger’s regressions with a two-step precision/variance correction, and tests for a decline in effect size over time.
Cross-Dataset Extraction Validation (extraction_validation.qmd) — Checks whether the two original datasets (Davies et al. 2020 and Jones & DuVal 2019) agree on the effect sizes of the studies they both cover, and re-extracts the worst disagreements from the source PDFs.

title: Mate-choice copying in non-human animals: an update of two meta-analyses
text:
Reproducibility & Environment
We conducted data preparation steps using R and Python, all statistical analyses are coded in R, and compiled dynamically using Quarto.
To ensure stability and smooth local execution:

R dependencies (metafor, orchaRd, ggplot2, patchwork, DT) are managed dynamically via pacman::p_load().
Interactive datatables are rendered with DT::datatable() for dynamic search and export capability.
Document states are frozen during compilation (freeze: auto) to ensure rendering stability.
All code blocks are folded by default but can be toggled using the “Show Code” buttons throughout the book chapters.

title: Literature Screening & Processing Pipeline
text:
Overview
This page documents all data decisions, processing steps, and planned analyses for the systematic review and meta-analysis of mate-choice copying (MCC) update across animal taxa.
Mate-choice copying occurs when an individual’s preference for a potential mate is influenced by observing the mate choices of others — a form of socially acquired preference that has been documented in fish, birds, mammals, and invertebrates.
This meta-analysis aims to:

Estimate the overall effect size for MCC across species.
Quantify between-study heterogeneity.
Test for moderating variables (sex, taxon, experimental design).

title: Literature Screening & Processing Pipeline
text:
Data Sources

Literature search
Records were retrieved from two major bibliographic databases and combined in Rayyan format for import into R:

Scopus — full-text and abstract search using Boolean terms covering mate choice, copying, non-independent mate selection, and social information use.
Web of Science (WoS) — parallel search with identical query structure.

The two exports were merged into a single file: data/0_literature_screening/combined_records_final.csv


Master dataset fields


Show R code
fields <- tibble::tribble(
    ~Field,        ~Description,                                          ~Completeness,
    "title",       "Article title (primary deduplication key)",           "~100%",

    "authors",     "Author list",                                         "~95%",

    "source_file", "Which database CSV contributed this record",          "100%",

title: Literature Screening & Processing Pipeline
text:
Processing Pipeline
All deduplication and screener-assignment logic lives in a single reproducible script:

scripts/0_literature_screening/1_deduplicate_records.R

The script was written in February 2026 using data.table for efficient in-memory processing of the 2,083-record dataset.

Step 1 — Load data


Show R code
rm(list = ls())

library(dplyr)
library(data.table)
library(stringr)
library(readr)

# Load the combined Scopus + Web of Science export (2,083 records before deduplication)
articles <- fread("data/0_literature_screening/combined_records_final.csv")




Step 2 — Title-based deduplication
Titles are normalised to a canonical lowercase form before matching. When duplicates are found, the highest-quality record is retained.
Quality score (0–3): one point each for having a non-empty abstract, a non-empty author list, and a non-empty DOI.


Show R code
# --- Normalise title ----------------------------------------------------------
articles[, title_lwr := str_to_lower(title)]
articles[, title_lwr := gsub("[[:punct:]]", " ", title_lwr)] # strip punctuation
articles[, title_lwr := gsub(" {2,}", " ", title_lwr)] # collapse whitespace
articles[, title_lwr := trimws(title_lwr)]

# --- Quality score (0–3) ------------------------------------------------------
articles[, has_abstract := ifelse(abstract == "" | is.na(abstract), 0L, 1L)]
articles[, has_authors := ifelse(authors == "" | is.na(authors), 0L, 1L)]
articles[, has_doi := ifelse(doi == "" | is.na(doi), 0L, 1L)]
articles[, quality := has_abstract + has_authors + has_doi]

# --- Concatenate source files per title group ---------------------------------
sources <- articles[,
    .(source_file = paste(sort(unique(source_file)), collapse = "; ")),
    by = .(title_lwr)
]

# --- Keep highest-quality record per title ------------------------------------
articles.title.dedupe <- articles[
    , .SD[which.max(quality), !c("source_file"), with = FALSE],
    by = .(title_lwr)
]

# --- Merge concatenated sources back in ---------------------------------------
articles.title.dedupe.mrg <- merge(
    articles.title.dedupe, sources,
    by = "title_lwr", all.x = TRUE
)



Data decisions — title deduplication








Decision
What was done
Rationale




Lowercase conversion
str_to_lower()
Catches case differences across databases


Punctuation removal
gsub("[[:punct:]]", " ", ...)
Handles hyphens, apostrophes, colons in titles


Whitespace collapse
gsub(" {2,}", " ", ...)
Normalises after punctuation removal


Quality scoring
abstract + authors + doi (0/1 each)
Retains the most information-rich duplicate


Source concatenation
paste(sort(unique(source_file)), collapse = "; ")
Full provenance preserved across all duplicates






Step 3 — DOI-based deduplication
A second pass on the DOI field catches articles in which titles differ slightly between database exports but resolve to the same paper. A custom helper function split_conc() uniquely concatenates source strings that were themselves already concatenated in the title-dedup step.


Show R code
# Helper: split already-concatenated source strings, deduplicate, re-join
split_conc <- function(x) {
    str_split(x, pattern = "; ") |>
        unlist() |>
        unique() |>
        sort() |>
        paste(collapse = "; ")
}

# Recalculate quality after title dedup (fields may have changed)
articles.title.dedupe.mrg[, has_abstract := ifelse(abstract == "" | is.na(abstract), 0L, 1L)]
articles.title.dedupe.mrg[, has_authors := ifelse(authors == "" | is.na(authors), 0L, 1L)]
articles.title.dedupe.mrg[, has_doi := ifelse(doi == "" | is.na(doi), 0L, 1L)]
articles.title.dedupe.mrg[, quality := has_abstract + has_authors + has_doi]

# Concatenate sources per DOI
sources <- articles.title.dedupe.mrg[,
    .(source_file = split_conc(source_file)),
    by = .(doi)
]

# Keep highest-quality record per DOI
articles.doi.dedupe <- articles.title.dedupe.mrg[
    , .SD[which.max(quality), !c("source_file"), with = FALSE],
    by = .(doi)
]

# Merge sources back in
articles.final <- merge(
    articles.doi.dedupe, sources,
    by = "doi", all.x = TRUE
)




Step 4 — Clean and append provenance to abstract
Intermediate processing columns are dropped, and the source_file string is appended to the abstract field so that provenance survives any downstream tool that does not retain a source_file column.


Show R code
articles.final[, abstract := paste0(abstract, " | ", source_file)]

articles.final <- articles.final[
    ,
    !c(
        "title_lwr", "n_title_matches",
        "has_abstract", "has_authors", "has_doi",
        "quality", "n_doi_matches", "source_file"
    )
]




Step 5 — Year filter: 2019 and later


Show R code
articles.final <- articles.final[year >= 2019, ]
message("Records after filter: ", nrow(articles.final))
message("Earliest year in dataset: ", min(articles.final$year))








ImportantData decision — year filter



Only records published in or after 2019 are retained for screening.
Rationale:

Aligns with the pre-registered scope of the review.
Pre-2019 records remain available in combined_records_final.csv and can be incorporated in sensitivity analyses.

The filter reduces the deduplicated pool to 495 articles.




Step 6 — Random shuffle and screener assignment
Articles are randomly shuffled before assignment to prevent ordering artefacts (e.g., alphabetical or chronological clustering within screener batches). A dynamic function generates near-equal assignment vectors for any team size.


Show R code
# Team structure
screeners <- c(
    "Aleksandra", "Aneta", "Anna", "Ayumi", "Cassidy", "Christine",
    "Erick", "Iwo", "Hao", "Jimuel", "Kyle", "Marija",
    "Santiago", "Sergio", "Shinichi", # 15 primary screeners
    "Losia", "Eduardo" # 2 secondary validators
)

primary_list <- setdiff(screeners, c("Eduardo", "Losia"))
secondary_list <- c("Eduardo", "Losia")

# Random shuffle (prevents date/alpha ordering bias)
set.seed(NULL) # no fixed seed — shuffled output files are the reproducible record
articles.final <- articles.final[sample(.N), ]

# Dynamic assignment: ceiling(total / n) reps, trimmed to total rows
create_assignments <- function(names, total_rows) {
    reps_needed <- ceiling(total_rows / length(names))
    full_vector <- rep(names, each = reps_needed)
    return(full_vector[seq_len(total_rows)])
}

n_total <- nrow(articles.final)

# Primary: ~33 articles per screener (495 / 15)
articles.final[, primary_screener := create_assignments(primary_list, n_total)]

# Secondary: ~248 articles per validator (495 / 2)
articles.final[, secondary_screener := create_assignments(secondary_list, n_total)]

# Verify balance
articles.final[, .(n = .N), by = .(primary_screener)]
articles.final[, .(n = .N), by = .(secondary_screener)]




Step 7 — Export per-screener CSVs
One CSV is written per primary screener to builds/0_literature_screening/deduplicated_and_randomly_split/. Each file retains both assignment columns for cross-referencing.


Show R code
invisible(lapply(
    unique(articles.final$primary_screener),
    function(x) {
        write_csv(
            articles.final[primary_screener == x, ],
            paste0("builds/0_literature_screening/deduplicated_and_randomly_split/", x, ".csv")
        )
    }
))

Inclusion criteria
Articles are included if they:

Report an experimental test of mate-choice copying (observer watches a demonstrator make a mate choice, then has the opportunity to choose between the same mates).
Provide sufficient quantitative data for effect-size extraction (means + variance, or counts, or test statistics with sample sizes).
Were published 2019 or later (pre-registration scope).
Are written in English.



Inter-rater reliability
Disagreements between primary and secondary screeners will be resolved by discussion or a designated tie-breaker.

title: Literature Screening & Processing Pipeline
text:
Data Extraction Pipeline
Included articles are sent to Google NotebookLM for AI-assisted data extraction via a four-step structured prompting protocol. All prompts are grounded by a MetaAnalysis_Reference_Sheet.pdf data dictionary containing few-shot examples of correct extractions.






WarningCache-bust rule



Each step must be run in a completely new NotebookLM chat window. Re-using a previous window causes the model to recycle earlier tables rather than reading the PDFs afresh.



Step 1 — Species & study identification
Goal: Establish taxonomic accuracy across all included papers.
Each PDF yields one row in a Markdown table with two columns:








Column
Format
Example




Study_ID
FirstAuthorLastName_Year_CamelCaseJournal
Belkina_2021_AnimalBehaviour


Species
Full binomial nomenclature
Poecilia reticulata



Common names alone are not accepted. The Study ID format is consistent across all four steps to serve as the primary join key.


Step 2 — Co-authorship & lab identification
Goal: Build a bipartite co-authorship network to identify “hub labs.”
Output is in long format — one row per author per paper — so that network edges can be constructed directly.







Column
Description




Study_ID
Join key


Author_Name
Full name


Author_Position
First / Last / Corresponding / Middle


Institutional_Affiliation
University or institute


Lab_Identifier
PILastName_Institution_City (e.g., Dukas_UniversityOfToronto_Toronto)









NoteLab identifier design



The last author is designated as the lab PI, and the same Lab_Identifier string is applied to every co-author row on the same paper. This links all authors to one “primary lab” node in the bipartite network, enabling correct degree-centrality calculation for hub-lab detection.




Step 3 — Trial-level data mapping
Goal: Map the internal experimental structure of each paper — how many distinct trials exist, where their data live, and whether subjects are independent.







Column
Description




Study_ID
Join key


Trial_ID
Experiment name/number (e.g., “Experiment 1”, “Treatment A”)


Trial_Description
1–2 sentence summary of manipulation


Sample_Size_N
Focal subjects in this trial


Subject_Independence
“Independent Cohort” or “Repeated Measures”


Control_Group_Shared
Yes/No + which trial (if Yes)


Data_Location
Exact page and figure/table reference (e.g., “Table 1, Page 5”)





Step 4 — Quantitative extraction (multi-agent pipeline)
Goal: Extract the actual numbers and cross-check them within a single structured prompt.

Three-role prompting protocol
The extraction prompt instructs the language model to perform three sequential roles on each paper, so that every number is pulled, checked, and reconciled before it is written out. These are roles played by one model, not three separate programs:

Role 1 — Extractor: pulls the raw statistics (means, variances, counts, and test statistics) directly from the Methods and Results text and tables.
Role 2 — Verifier: cross-checks the extracted numbers, verifies sample independence, flags any standard-deviation vs. standard-error confusion, and re-reads the text to confirm no secondary trials were missed.
Role 3 — Arbiter: resolves any discrepancies between the Extractor and Verifier, standardises the reporting, and outputs the final data row.

The Verifier role checks explicitly for three systematic extraction errors:

SD/SE confusion — a common error in manual extraction that biases heterogeneity estimates if uncorrected.
Sample independence — flags whether the N used is independent across trials or from repeated measures on the same subjects.
Missing trials — re-reads the Methods to confirm no secondary experiments were missed by Agent 1.



Final output columns







Column
Content




Study_ID
Author_Year_JournalName


Trial_ID
Specific experiment or condition


Response_Metric
What was measured


Validated_Exp_Group_Data
Mean ± SD/SE, Counts, or Test Stat


Validated_Control_Group_Data
Mean ± SD/SE, Counts, or NA


SD_or_SE
Explicit variance metric label


Agent1_Initial_Pull
Brief summary of original extraction


Agent2_Verification_Notes
Corrections made, or “No missing trials found”


Verification_Location
Exact page and table/figure number

title: Literature Screening & Processing Pipeline
text:
Effect Size Calculation
Following data extraction, effect sizes and their sampling variances are calculated using the reproducible Python pipeline located at scripts/1_effect_size_calculation_pipeline/calculate_effect_sizes.py.
The raw data is stored in the Excel spreadsheet data/1_effect_size_calculation_pipeline/Data Extraction Mate Choice Meta Analysis.xlsx inside the EduardoExtraction sheet. To support tracking, each row contains an identifierEffectSizeID column containing a unique sequential identifier for each control–experimental comparison (e.g., es0001, es0002). The Python pipeline dynamically resolves column locations by scanning the header row, computes the following effect size metrics, and saves them back to the spreadsheet:

1. Hedges’ \(g\) (Bias-Corrected Standardised Mean Difference)
Calculated depending on the data type extracted:

From Means & SDs: Using control and treatment means, standard deviations (or standard errors converted using \(SD = SE \times \sqrt{n}\)), and sample sizes.
From \(t\)-statistics: Supported for both paired/one-sample designs (Case 2, in the Python script) and independent-sample designs (Case 3, assuming an equal sample split if individual group sizes are unknown).



2. Odds Ratio (OR)
Calculated for categorical outcomes from \(2 \times 2\) contingency tables (\(A\), \(B\), \(C\), \(D\)), where the sampling variance of the log odds ratio is approximated using the delta method.


3. Odds Ratio \(\leftrightarrow\) Hedges’ \(g\) Cross-Conversion
For rows containing only one of the two metrics, the missing metric is derived using the logistic-normal approximation (Chinn 2000; Borenstein et al. 2011):

\(\text{OR} \rightarrow \text{Hedges' } g\): \(d = \ln(\text{OR}) \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{\pi}\) and then bias-corrected to \(g\) using the \(J\) correction factor.
\(\text{Hedges' } g \rightarrow \text{OR}\): \(\ln(\text{OR}) = g \times \frac{\pi}{\sqrt{3}}\) and \(\text{Var}(\ln\text{OR}) = \text{Var}(g) \times \frac{\pi^2}{3}\).

To execute the calculations and update the workbook, run:
python scripts/1_effect_size_calculation_pipeline/calculate_effect_sizes.py

title: Literature Screening & Processing Pipeline
text:
Planned Analyses
All models will be implemented in R. Our pre-registration details are available in Santos et al. (2026).

Meta-analytic models
Multi-level random-effects meta-analysis to estimate the overall effect size for MCC, accounting for trials nested within papers nested within labs.
Package: metafor.
Heterogeneity will be quantified with \(I^2\) and \(\tau^2\) at each level.


Phylogenetic comparative methods
Species non-independence is controlled via a phylogenetic covariance matrix incorporated into the meta-analytic model (metafor + ape). This accounts for the possibility that closely related species share similar MCC propensity due to shared evolutionary history.


Publication bias
Funnel plot asymmetry, and Egger’s regression test to assess publication bias. Time-lag bias will be examined by plotting effect size against publication year.


Moderator analyses
Meta-regression models will test whether effect sizes vary by:

Sex of the copying individual (female vs. male)
Taxonomic group (fish, bird, mammal, invertebrate)
Experimental design (before-and-after vs. no pre-test)
Copying mechanism (individual vs. generalized)



Sensitivity analyses

Inclusion of pre-2019 records.
Leave-one-out analysis for influential studies.







Borenstein, Michael, Larry V. Hedges, Julian P. T. Higgins, and Hannah R. Rothstein. 2011. Introduction to Meta-Analysis. John Wiley & Sons. https://books.google.com?id=JQg9jdrq26wC.


Chinn, S. 2000. “A Simple Method for Converting an Odds Ratio to Effect Size for Use in Meta-Analysis.” Statistics in Medicine 19 (22): 3127–31. https://doi.org/10.1002/1097-0258(20001130)19:22<3127::aid-sim784>3.0.co;2-m.


Santos, Eduardo, Shinichi Nakagawa, and Malgorzata Lagisz. 2026. “Mate-Choice Copying in Non-Human Animals: Protocol for the Update of Two Meta-Analyses [Protocol].” OSF, ahead of print. https://doi.org/10.17605/OSF.IO/VFAHK.

title: Overall Effect Size Calculation & Synthesis
text:
Introduction & Project Update
This chapter calculates the overall effect size for the updated mate-choice copying (MCC) meta-analysis.
We combine three sources of data:
The R code below puts these sources onto common scales and then reports the meta-analytic summary for three subsets, so the new and original data can be compared:
crumbs: 3  Overall Effect Size Calculation & Synthesis

title: Overall Effect Size Calculation & Synthesis
text:
The new extraction: our newly extracted and verified dataset of recent studies.
Original continuous dataset (Davies et al. (2020)): standardised mean differences (Hedges’ \(d\)) for continuous outcomes.
Original binary dataset (Jones and DuVal (2019)): log odds ratios (\(\ln\text{OR}\)) for binary (choice) outcomes.



New extraction only: the newly extracted dataset on its own.
Original only: the previously published data on its own.
Combined: the new and original data pooled together — the basis for the updated overall estimates.
crumbs: 3  Overall Effect Size Calculation & Synthesis

title: Overall Effect Size Calculation & Synthesis
text:
Data Loading and Alignment
We read the three datasets, perform data type conversions, extract species epithets for taxonomic control, and align columns into unified Hedges’ \(g\) and Odds Ratio datasets.


Show R code
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Load Datasets
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Paths to legacy and new datasets
path_new <- here::here("data", "1_effect_size_calculation_pipeline", "Data Extraction Mate Choice Meta Analysis.xlsx")
path_davies <- here::here("data", "original_meta_analyses_datasets", "Davies_et_al_2020_Final_data.xlsx")
path_jones <- here::here("data", "original_meta_analyses_datasets", "Jones_DuVal_2019_data.CSV")

# A. Read new extraction dataset (EduardoExtraction sheet)
db_new_raw <- readxl::read_excel(path_new, sheet = "EduardoExtraction")

# B. Read Davies et al. 2020 (Original continuous dataset)
db_davies_raw <- readxl::read_excel(path_davies, sheet = "Data")

# C. Read Jones & DuVal 2019 (Original binary dataset)
db_jones_raw <- readr::read_csv(path_jones)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1b. Species-name reconciliation + phylogenetic correlation matrices
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# The phylogenetic correlation matrices (built in scripts/2_phylogeny/
# build_phylogeny.R) are indexed by FULL species binomials. The mappings below
# reproduce that script's reconciliation so the `species_phylo` grouping in the
# effect-size data matches the tree tip labels (and matrix dimnames) exactly.
recode_new    <- c("Taeniopygia guttata castanotis" = "Taeniopygia guttata")
recode_davies <- c("Schizocosa" = "Schizocosa ocreata")

# Jones & DuVal store only the species EPITHET; rebuild full binomials from
# Family + epithet (identical to build_phylogeny.R).
jones_binomial <- tibble::tribble(
    ~family,            ~epithet,        ~species_phylo,
    "Adrianichthyidae", "latipes",       "Oryzias latipes",
    "Blenniidae",       "nitidus",       "Rhabdoblennius nitidus",
    "Gasterosteidae",   "aculeatus",     "Gasterosteus aculeatus",
    "Gobiidae",         "minutu",        "Pomatoschistus minutus",
    "Percidae",         "olmstedi",      "Etheostoma olmstedi",
    "Poeciliidae",      "latipinna",     "Poecilia latipinna",
    "Poeciliidae",      "nigrofasciata", "Limia nigrofasciata",
    "Poeciliidae",      "perugiae",      "Limia perugiae",
    "Poeciliidae",      "reticulata",    "Poecilia reticulata",
    "Pomacentridae",    "leucogaster",   "Amblyglyphidodon leucogaster",
    "Lycosidae",        "ocreata",       "Schizocosa ocreata",
    "Estrildidae",      "guttata",       "Taeniopygia guttata",
    "Icteridae",        "ater",          "Molothrus ater",
    "Muscicapidae",     "hypoleuca",     "Ficedula hypoleuca",
    "Drosophilidae",    "melanogaster",  "Drosophila melanogaster",
    "Drosophilidae",    "serrata",       "Drosophila serrata",
    "Ocypodidae",       "mjoebergi",     "Uca mjoebergi"
)

# Phylogenetic correlation matrices (full binomials as dimnames). Loaded
# defensively: if the phylogeny build has not been run, these are NULL and the
# models gracefully fall back to a non-phylogenetic species random effect only.
read_phylo <- function(f) {
    p <- here::here("outputs", "2_phylogeny", f)
    if (file.exists(p)) readRDS(p) else NULL
}
phylo_cor_new    <- read_phylo("phylo_cor_new_extraction.rds") # new-extraction tree
phylo_cor_hedges <- read_phylo("phylo_cor_hedges_model.rds")   # combined Hedges' g species
phylo_cor_or     <- read_phylo("phylo_cor_or_model.rds")       # combined log OR species

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. Process and Filter New Extraction
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
db_new <- db_new_raw %>%
    filter(is.na(RemoveFromDataset) | RemoveFromDataset != "Yes") %>%
    mutate(
        effectSizeCalculatedHedgesD = as.numeric(effectSizeCalculatedHedgesD),
        effectSizeCalculatedHedgesDVariance = as.numeric(effectSizeCalculatedHedgesDVariance),
        effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatio = as.numeric(effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatio),
        effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatioVariance = as.numeric(effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatioVariance),
        logOddsRatio = log(effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatio),
        logOddsRatioVariance = effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatioVariance
    )

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. Align Datasets for Hedges' g (Continuous SMD Analysis)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

# A. Hedges' g from New Extraction
hedges_new <- db_new %>%
    filter(!is.na(effectSizeCalculatedHedgesD), !is.na(effectSizeCalculatedHedgesDVariance)) %>%
    select(
        yi = effectSizeCalculatedHedgesD,
        vi = effectSizeCalculatedHedgesDVariance,
        identifierStudyId,
        identifierEffectSizeID,
        taxonomySpecies
    ) %>%
    mutate(
        identifierStudyId = as.character(identifierStudyId),
        identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(identifierEffectSizeID),
        # Full binomial for the phylogeny (matches tree tip labels / matrix dimnames)
        species_phylo = dplyr::recode(str_squish(as.character(taxonomySpecies)), !!!recode_new),
        # Extract species epithet (last word) and make lowercase for cross-dataset mapping
        taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(word(as.character(taxonomySpecies), -1)),
        Dataset_Source = "New Extraction (Santos)"
    )

# B. Hedges' g from Davies et al. 2020 (Original)
hedges_davies <- db_davies_raw %>%
    mutate(
        Hedges_d_directional = as.numeric(Hedges_d_directional),
        Variance = as.numeric(Variance)
    ) %>%
    filter(!is.na(Hedges_d_directional), !is.na(Variance), Variance > 0) %>%
    select(
        yi = Hedges_d_directional,
        vi = Variance,
        identifierStudyId = Author,
        identifierEffectSizeID = Effect_size_no,
        taxonomySpecies = Species_latin
    ) %>%
    mutate(
        identifierStudyId = as.character(identifierStudyId),
        identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(identifierEffectSizeID),
        # Full binomial for the phylogeny (matches tree tip labels / matrix dimnames)
        species_phylo = dplyr::recode(str_squish(as.character(taxonomySpecies)), !!!recode_davies),
        # Extract species epithet (last word) and make lowercase
        taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(word(as.character(taxonomySpecies), -1)),
        Dataset_Source = "Original (Davies 2020)"
    )

# C. Unified Hedges' g Dataset
db_hedges <- bind_rows(hedges_new, hedges_davies) %>%
    mutate(across(c(identifierStudyId, taxonomySpecies, species_phylo, Dataset_Source), as.factor))

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 4. Align Datasets for Odds Ratio (Binary Analysis)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------

# A. Log Odds Ratio from New Extraction
or_new <- db_new %>%
    filter(!is.na(logOddsRatio), !is.na(logOddsRatioVariance)) %>%
    select(
        yi = logOddsRatio,
        vi = logOddsRatioVariance,
        identifierStudyId,
        identifierEffectSizeID,
        taxonomySpecies
    ) %>%
    mutate(
        identifierStudyId = as.character(identifierStudyId),
        identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(identifierEffectSizeID),
        # Full binomial for the phylogeny (matches tree tip labels / matrix dimnames)
        species_phylo = dplyr::recode(str_squish(as.character(taxonomySpecies)), !!!recode_new),
        taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(word(as.character(taxonomySpecies), -1)),
        Dataset_Source = "New Extraction (Santos)"
    )

# B. Log Odds Ratio from Jones & DuVal 2019 (Original)
or_jones <- db_jones_raw %>%
    filter(!is.na(lnOR), !is.na(VlnOR), VlnOR > 0) %>%
    mutate(
        family  = str_squish(as.character(Family)),
        epithet = str_squish(as.character(Species))
    ) %>%
    # Rebuild full binomial (species_phylo) from Family + epithet
    left_join(jones_binomial, by = c("family", "epithet")) %>%
    select(
        yi = lnOR,
        vi = VlnOR,
        identifierStudyId = study,
        identifierEffectSizeID = study_Exp,
        taxonomySpecies = Species,
        species_phylo
    ) %>%
    mutate(
        identifierStudyId = as.character(identifierStudyId),
        identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(identifierEffectSizeID),
        taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(as.character(taxonomySpecies)),
        Dataset_Source = "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)"
    )

# Sanity check: every Jones epithet should map to a full binomial
if (any(is.na(or_jones$species_phylo))) {
    warning(
        "Unmapped Jones epithets (phylogeny): ",
        paste(unique(or_jones$taxonomySpecies[is.na(or_jones$species_phylo)]), collapse = ", ")
    )
}

# C. Unified Log Odds Ratio Dataset
db_or <- bind_rows(or_new, or_jones) %>%
    mutate(across(c(identifierStudyId, taxonomySpecies, species_phylo, Dataset_Source), as.factor))

title: Overall Effect Size Calculation & Synthesis
text:
Model Fitting Function
This helper function handles the variance–covariance (VCV) matrix construction and fits a multilevel random-effects model using rma.mv() in metafor.

Variance–Covariance: We construct a VCV matrix via vcalc() to handle dependencies among multiple effect sizes within the same study (assuming \(r = 0.5\) for nested measurements).
Random intercepts are modeled for:

Study (identifierStudyId)
Individual trial/effect size (identifierEffectSizeID)
Species (taxonomySpecies) — the non-phylogenetic species effect, capturing species-specific deviations not explained by shared ancestry
Phylogeny (species_phylo) — a phylogenetic random effect supplied with a species-by-species correlation matrix (R = list(species_phylo = phylo_cor)) derived from the Open Tree of Life topology with Grafen branch lengths (see scripts/2_phylogeny/build_phylogeny.R). This partitions the among-species variance into a component attributable to phylogenetic relatedness and a residual species component.


The phylogenetic term is added to the uncorrected models only (for the New, Original, and Combined datasets); the small-study bias-corrected (Egger-type) models retain the original structure. When a phylogenetic correlation matrix is unavailable, the fit gracefully falls back to the non-phylogenetic structure.


Show R code
fit_overall_mv <- function(dat, yi = "yi", vi = "vi", cluster_var = "identifierStudyId", obs_var = "identifierEffectSizeID", rho = 0.5, phylo_cor = NULL, phylo_var = "species_phylo") {
    # Remove rows with missing values
    dat2 <- dat %>%
        filter(!is.na(.data[[yi]]), !is.na(.data[[vi]]), .data[[vi]] > 0)

    if (nrow(dat2) == 0) {
        message("No complete cases found.")
        return(NULL)
    }

    # 1. Build VCV Matrix
    VCV <- metafor::vcalc(
        vi = dat2[[vi]],
        cluster = dat2[[cluster_var]],
        obs = dat2[[obs_var]],
        rho = rho,
        data = dat2
    )

    # 2. Assemble random-effects structure. Always include study, effect-size,
    #    and a (non-phylogenetic) species intercept. If a phylogenetic
    #    correlation matrix is supplied AND every species level is present in it,
    #    add a phylogenetic random effect with R = subset-of-correlation-matrix.
    random_list <- list(
        ~ 1 | identifierStudyId,
        ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID,
        ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies
    )
    R_arg <- NULL
    used_phylo <- FALSE

    if (!is.null(phylo_cor) && phylo_var %in% colnames(dat2)) {
        sp <- unique(as.character(dat2[[phylo_var]]))
        missing_sp <- setdiff(sp, rownames(phylo_cor))
        if (length(missing_sp) == 0) {
            # Subset the correlation matrix to the species actually present so the
            # dimnames match the grouping levels exactly. Also drop any stale
            # (unused) factor levels on the grouping column so metafor's level
            # set matches the R-matrix dimnames.
            dat2[[phylo_var]] <- factor(as.character(dat2[[phylo_var]]))
            Csub <- phylo_cor[sp, sp, drop = FALSE]
            random_list <- c(random_list, list(stats::as.formula(paste0("~ 1 | ", phylo_var))))
            R_arg <- stats::setNames(list(Csub), phylo_var)
            used_phylo <- TRUE
        } else {
            warning(
                "Phylogenetic random effect skipped (species absent from correlation matrix): ",
                paste(missing_sp, collapse = ", ")
            )
        }
    }

    # 3. Fit Multi-Level Random-Effects Model
    mod <- metafor::rma.mv(
        yi = dat2[[yi]],
        V = VCV,
        random = random_list,
        R = R_arg,
        test = "t",
        method = "REML",
        sparse = TRUE,
        data = dat2
    )

    list(data = dat2, VCV = VCV, model = mod, used_phylo = used_phylo)
}

# Helper function to fit bias-corrected multilevel models with a continuous moderator (Egger-type)
fit_corrected_mv <- function(dat, yi = "yi", vi = "vi", moderator = "vi", cluster_var = "identifierStudyId", obs_var = "identifierEffectSizeID", rho = 0.5) {
    # Remove rows with missing values
    dat2 <- dat %>%
        filter(!is.na(.data[[yi]]), !is.na(.data[[vi]]), .data[[vi]] > 0)

    if (nrow(dat2) == 0) {
        message("No complete cases found.")
        return(NULL)
    }

    if (moderator == "se" && !"se" %in% colnames(dat2)) {
        dat2 <- dat2 %>% mutate(se = sqrt(vi))
    }

    # 1. Build VCV Matrix
    VCV <- metafor::vcalc(
        vi = dat2[[vi]],
        cluster = dat2[[cluster_var]],
        obs = dat2[[obs_var]],
        rho = rho,
        data = dat2
    )

    # 2. Fit Multi-Level Egger Model using dynamic formula
    formula_str <- paste("~ 1 +", moderator)
    mod <- metafor::rma.mv(
        yi = dat2[[yi]],
        V = VCV,
        mods = as.formula(formula_str),
        random = list(
            ~ 1 | identifierStudyId,
            ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID,
            ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies
        ),
        test = "t",
        method = "REML",
        sparse = TRUE,
        data = dat2
    )

    list(data = dat2, VCV = VCV, model = mod)
}

# Helper function to add Mean (95% CI) labels dynamically one line above k-values in orchard plots
add_ci_labels <- function(plot_obj, orchard_results) {
    est <- sprintf("%.2f", orchard_results$mod_table$estimate)
    ci_l <- sprintf("%.2f", orchard_results$mod_table$lowerCL)
    ci_u <- sprintf("%.2f", orchard_results$mod_table$upperCL)

    orig_labels <- plot_obj$layers[[6]]$aes_params$label

    new_labels <- c()
    for (i in 1:length(orig_labels)) {
        # Extract the original sample size numbers using regex
        nums <- regmatches(orig_labels[i], gregexpr("[0-9]+", orig_labels[i]))[[1]]
        if (length(nums) >= 2) {
            k_val <- nums[1]
            n_val <- nums[2]
            k_text <- paste0("k = ", k_val, " (", n_val, ")")
        } else {
            k_text <- orig_labels[i]
        }
        # Place Mean (95% CI) on the first line and k = xx (xx) on the second line
        new_labels[i] <- paste0(est[i], " [", ci_l[i], ", ", ci_u[i], "]\n", k_text)
    }

    plot_obj$layers[[6]]$aes_params$label <- new_labels
    plot_obj$layers[[6]]$geom_params$parse <- FALSE
    plot_obj$layers[[6]]$aes_params$hjust <- "right"
    plot_obj$layers[[6]]$aes_params$vjust <- 0.5
    plot_obj$layers[[6]]$aes_params$size <- 3.0 # Slightly smaller font size to fit better

    plot_obj
}

text:
Multi-Level Synthesis Results
We fit the multilevel meta-analysis across all three database splits (New Only, Original Only, and Combined) for both effect size classes.


Show R code
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# HEDGES' G MODELS (UNCORRECTED) — now incorporating phylogenetic relatedness
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# New-only uses the new-extraction tree; Original-only and Combined use the
# combined Hedges' g tree (Davies species are a subset of the combined tree).
res_hedges_new <- fit_overall_mv(db_hedges %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "New Extraction (Santos)"), phylo_cor = phylo_cor_new)
res_hedges_orig <- fit_overall_mv(db_hedges %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "Original (Davies 2020)"), phylo_cor = phylo_cor_hedges)
res_hedges_comb <- fit_overall_mv(db_hedges, phylo_cor = phylo_cor_hedges)

# HEDGES' G MODELS (BIAS-CORRECTED)
res_hedges_new_corr <- fit_corrected_mv(db_hedges %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "New Extraction (Santos)"), moderator = "vi")
res_hedges_orig_corr <- fit_corrected_mv(db_hedges %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "Original (Davies 2020)"), moderator = "vi")
res_hedges_comb_corr <- fit_corrected_mv(db_hedges, moderator = "vi")

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# ODDS RATIO MODELS (UNCORRECTED) — now incorporating phylogenetic relatedness
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# New-only uses the new-extraction tree; Original-only and Combined use the
# combined log OR tree (Jones species are a subset of the combined tree).
res_or_new <- fit_overall_mv(db_or %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "New Extraction (Santos)"), phylo_cor = phylo_cor_new)
res_or_orig <- fit_overall_mv(db_or %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)"), phylo_cor = phylo_cor_or)
res_or_comb <- fit_overall_mv(db_or, phylo_cor = phylo_cor_or)

# ODDS RATIO MODELS (BIAS-CORRECTED)
res_or_new_corr <- fit_corrected_mv(db_or %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "New Extraction (Santos)"), moderator = "vi")
res_or_orig_corr <- fit_corrected_mv(db_or %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)"), moderator = "vi")
res_or_comb_corr <- fit_corrected_mv(db_or, moderator = "vi")

Results and Visualizations

Hedges’ \(g\) (Continuous outcomes)Odds Ratio (Binary outcomes)



Uncorrected Overall EffectBias-Corrected Overall EffectSide-by-Side ComparisonDetailed Model Statistics


This plot displays the standard multi-level meta-analytic summaries across the three splits (New Only, Original Only, and Combined) without adjusting for publication bias. Points represent individual effect sizes colored by their data source.


Show R code
if (!is.null(res_hedges_new) && !is.null(res_hedges_orig) && !is.null(res_hedges_comb)) {
    # 1. Extract S3 result objects using mod_results
    h_new <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_hedges_new$model, group = "identifierStudyId")
    h_orig <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_hedges_orig$model, group = "identifierStudyId")
    h_comb <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_hedges_comb$model, group = "identifierStudyId")

    # 2. Clone S3 result objects to lists for manual editing
    class(h_new) <- "list"
    h_new$mod_table$name <- "New Extraction (Santos)"
    h_new$data$moderator <- "New Extraction (Santos)"
    class(h_new) <- c("orchard", "data.frame")

    class(h_orig) <- "list"
    h_orig$mod_table$name <- "Original (Davies 2020)"
    h_orig$data$moderator <- "Original (Davies 2020)"
    class(h_orig) <- c("orchard", "data.frame")

    class(h_comb) <- "list"
    h_comb$mod_table$name <- "Combined"
    h_comb$data$moderator <- "Combined"
    class(h_comb) <- c("orchard", "data.frame")

    # 3. Merge model results
    sub_hedges <- orchaRd::submerge(h_new, h_orig, h_comb)

    # 4. Add point-level real source mapping
    class(sub_hedges) <- "list"
    sub_hedges$data$real_source <- c(
        rep("New Extraction (Santos)", nrow(h_new$data)),
        rep("Original (Davies 2020)", nrow(h_orig$data)),
        rep("New Extraction (Santos)", nrow(h_new$data)),
        rep("Original (Davies 2020)", nrow(h_orig$data))
    )
    class(sub_hedges) <- c("orchard", "data.frame")

    # 4. Generate Plot
    p_hedges <- orchaRd::orchard_plot(sub_hedges, xlab = "Hedges' g", flip = TRUE)

    # 5. Customize color mapping for points
    p_hedges$layers[[1]]$mapping$colour <- ggplot2::aes(colour = real_source)$colour
    p_hedges$layers[[1]]$mapping$fill <- ggplot2::aes(fill = real_source)$fill

    # 6. Apply themes & custom palette (colorblind friendly orange/blue)
    p_hedges <- p_hedges +
        ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Davies 2020)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") +
        ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Davies 2020)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") +
        ggplot2::ggtitle("Overall Synthesis — Hedges' g Estimates (Uncorrected)") +
        ggplot2::theme(
            legend.position = c(0.98, 0.02),
            legend.justification = c("right", "bottom"),
            legend.background = ggplot2::element_rect(fill = scales::alpha("white", 0.75), color = "grey90"),
            legend.box = "vertical",
            legend.margin = ggplot2::margin(t = 4, r = 4, b = 4, l = 4),
            legend.title = ggplot2::element_text(size = 9, face = "bold"),
            legend.text = ggplot2::element_text(size = 8)
        )

    # Add Mean (95% CI) and k labels
    p_hedges <- add_ci_labels(p_hedges, sub_hedges)

    print(p_hedges)
}











This plot displays the small-study publication bias corrected meta-analytic summaries (evaluated at \(\text{V} = 0\), representing the theoretical estimate for a study of infinite sample size/precision) using the two-step Egger-type meta-regression framework. Points represent the same raw effect sizes.


Show R code
if (!is.null(res_hedges_new_corr) && !is.null(res_hedges_orig_corr) && !is.null(res_hedges_comb_corr)) {
    # 1. Extract S3 result objects using mod_results at vi = 0
    h_new_corr <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_hedges_new_corr$model, group = "identifierStudyId", at = list(vi = 0), mod = "1")
    h_orig_corr <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_hedges_orig_corr$model, group = "identifierStudyId", at = list(vi = 0), mod = "1")
    h_comb_corr <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_hedges_comb_corr$model, group = "identifierStudyId", at = list(vi = 0), mod = "1")

    # 2. Clone S3 result objects to lists for manual editing
    class(h_new_corr) <- "list"
    h_new_corr$mod_table$name <- "New Extraction (Santos)"
    h_new_corr$data$moderator <- "New Extraction (Santos)"
    class(h_new_corr) <- c("orchard", "data.frame")

    class(h_orig_corr) <- "list"
    h_orig_corr$mod_table$name <- "Original (Davies 2020)"
    h_orig_corr$data$moderator <- "Original (Davies 2020)"
    class(h_orig_corr) <- c("orchard", "data.frame")

    class(h_comb_corr) <- "list"
    h_comb_corr$mod_table$name <- "Combined"
    h_comb_corr$data$moderator <- "Combined"
    class(h_comb_corr) <- c("orchard", "data.frame")

    # 3. Merge model results
    sub_hedges_corr <- orchaRd::submerge(h_new_corr, h_orig_corr, h_comb_corr)

if (!is.null(res_or_new) && !is.null(res_or_orig) && !is.null(res_or_comb)) {
    # 1. Extract S3 result objects using mod_results
    o_new <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_or_new$model, group = "identifierStudyId")
    o_orig <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_or_orig$model, group = "identifierStudyId")
    o_comb <- orchaRd::mod_results(res_or_comb$model, group = "identifierStudyId")

    # 2. Clone S3 result objects to lists for manual editing
    class(o_new) <- "list"
    o_new$mod_table$name <- "New Extraction (Santos)"
    o_new$data$moderator <- "New Extraction (Santos)"
    class(o_new) <- c("orchard", "data.frame")

    class(o_orig) <- "list"
    o_orig$mod_table$name <- "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)"
    o_orig$data$moderator <- "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)"
    class(o_orig) <- c("orchard", "data.frame")

    class(o_comb) <- "list"
    o_comb$mod_table$name <- "Combined"
    o_comb$data$moderator <- "Combined"
    class(o_comb) <- c("orchard", "data.frame")

    # 3. Merge model results
    sub_or <- orchaRd::submerge(o_new, o_orig, o_comb)

    # 4. Add point-level real source mapping
    class(sub_or) <- "list"
    sub_or$data$real_source <- c(
        rep("New Extraction (Santos)", nrow(o_new$data)),
        rep("Original (Jones & DuVal)", nrow(o_orig$data)),
        rep("New Extraction (Santos)", nrow(o_new$data)),
        rep("Original (Jones & DuVal)", nrow(o_orig$data))
    )
    class(sub_or) <- c("orchard", "data.frame")

    # 4. Generate Plot
    p_or <- orchaRd::orchard_plot(sub_or, xlab = "Log Odds Ratio (lnOR)", flip = TRUE)

    # 5. Customize color mapping for points
    p_or$layers[[1]]$mapping$colour <- ggplot2::aes(colour = real_source)$colour
    p_or$layers[[1]]$mapping$fill <- ggplot2::aes(fill = real_source)$fill

    # 6. Apply themes & custom palette (colorblind friendly orange/blue)
    p_or <- p_or +
        ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Jones & DuVal)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") +
        ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Jones & DuVal)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") +
        ggplot2::ggtitle("Overall Synthesis — Log Odds Ratio Estimates (Uncorrected)") +
        ggplot2::theme(
            legend.position = c(0.98, 0.02),

            legend.background = ggplot2::element_rect(fill = scales::alpha("white", 0.75), color = "grey90"),
            legend.box = "vertical",
            legend.margin = ggplot2::margin(t = 4, r = 4, b = 4, l = 4),
            legend.title = ggplot2::element_text(size = 9, face = "bold"),
            legend.text = ggplot2::element_text(size = 8)
        )

    # Add Mean (95% CI) and k labels
    p_or <- add_ci_labels(p_or, sub_or)

    print(p_or)
}

crumbs: 3  Overall Effect Size Calculation & Synthesis

title: Overall Effect Size Calculation & Synthesis
text:
Combined Correction Comparison
The tabs above present the uncorrected and bias-corrected estimates on separate plots. To make the comparison easier to read, we produce two figures — one for Hedges’ \(g\) and one for the log odds ratio — using the same orchaRd::pub_bias_plot() approach as the publication-bias chapter. Each figure stacks the three model levels (New Extraction, Original, Combined) as rows; within each row a single orchard trunk shows the uncorrected multilevel mean drawn over its raw effect sizes once, with the two corrected means overlaid on top. The raw points are coloured by data source and the legend is boxed in the corner, matching the side-by-side comparison plots above.
The three estimates shown per subplot are:

Uncorrected (the trunk) — the multilevel rma.mv mean.
Bias-Corrected (Nakagawa et al. (2022)); purple overlay — the intercept of the multilevel meta-regression with sampling variance (\(v_i\)) as a moderator, evaluated at \(v_i = 0\) (the effect a hypothetical infinitely-precise study would report).
Bias-Robust (Yang et al. (2024)); green overlay — a fixed-/common-effect model with cluster-robust (CR2) variance estimation clustered by study.



Show R code
# Yang et al. (2024) bias-robust estimate (fixed-effect + CR2 cluster-robust),
# fitted for each database split. Mirrors the construction in publication_bias.qmd
# but applied to New, Original, and Combined subsets.
fit_bias_robust <- function(dat, yi = "yi", vi = "vi", cluster_var = "identifierStudyId") {
    dat2 <- dat %>% filter(!is.na(.data[[yi]]), !is.na(.data[[vi]]), .data[[vi]] > 0)
    if (nrow(dat2) == 0) return(NULL)
    tryCatch({
        fe <- metafor::rma(yi = dat2[[yi]], vi = dat2[[vi]], method = "FE", test = "t")
        metafor::robust(fe, cluster = dat2[[cluster_var]], clubSandwich = TRUE)
    }, error = function(e) {
        warning("Bias-robust fit failed: ", conditionMessage(e)); NULL
    })
}

rob_hedges_new  <- fit_bias_robust(db_hedges %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "New Extraction (Santos)"))
rob_hedges_orig <- fit_bias_robust(db_hedges %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "Original (Davies 2020)"))
rob_hedges_comb <- fit_bias_robust(db_hedges)

rob_or_new  <- fit_bias_robust(db_or %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "New Extraction (Santos)"))
rob_or_orig <- fit_bias_robust(db_or %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)"))
rob_or_comb <- fit_bias_robust(db_or)




Show R code
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Subplot builder: one orchard trunk per model level, styled to match the
# `hedges_comparison_plot` aesthetics — raw points coloured by data source
# (colourblind orange/blue) with the boxed corner legend.
#
# The two corrected means are overlaid by hand (rather than via
# orchaRd::pub_bias_plot(), whose colours are hardcoded red/blue and whose text
# sits on top of the data) so that we can:
#   * use overlay colours that stand out from the orange/blue raw points
#     (green = Bias-Robust / Yang 2024, purple = Bias-Corrected / Nakagawa 2022);
#   * right-align the estimate text on the far side of the panel, kept inside
#     the plot box;
#   * share a common x-axis (effect-size) range across the stacked panels.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
source_palette <- c(
    "New Extraction (Santos)"       = "#D55E00",
    "Original (Davies 2020)"        = "#0072B2",
    "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)" = "#0072B2"
)

# Overlay colours — chosen to be distinct from the orange/blue raw points and
# colourblind-distinguishable (Okabe-Ito green + a strong violet).
yang_col <- "#009E73"  # Bias-Robust (Yang et al. 2024)
naka_col <- "#7B2D8E"  # Bias-Corrected (Nakagawa et al. 2022)

# Extract the corrected meta-analytic mean + 95% CI from an (intercept-only or
# vi-moderated) rma / rma.mv model — same intercept used by orchaRd::get_ints_dat.
corrected_est <- function(model) {
    b <- tryCatch(as.numeric(model$b["intrcpt", ]),
                  error = function(e) as.numeric(model$b[1]))
    data.frame(name = "Intrcpt", pred = b,
               ci.lb = model$ci.lb[1], ci.ub = model$ci.ub[1])
}

# src: either a single data-source label (recycled) or a per-point character
# vector matching the order of the model's effect sizes (used for Combined).
# xlim: optional c(lo, hi) shared effect-size range for axis alignment; when
# supplied, the estimate text is right-aligned to xlim[2] (inside the box).
make_pub_bias_subplot <- function(unc_res, corr_res, rob_model, src, xlab, title, xlim = NULL) {
    if (is.null(unc_res) || is.null(unc_res$model) ||
        is.null(corr_res) || is.null(corr_res$model) || is.null(rob_model)) {
        return(NULL)
    }
    # Single-trunk orchard result; attach the true per-point data source.
    h <- orchaRd::mod_results(unc_res$model, group = "identifierStudyId")
    if (length(src) == 1) src <- rep(src, nrow(h$data))
    class(h) <- "list"
    h$data$real_source <- src
    class(h) <- c("orchard", "data.frame")

    # k/g labels to the left so the right margin is free for the estimate text.
    # Widen point.size range so bubble area tracks precision more visibly.
    # trunk.size = 1 -> point estimate drawn at size 4 (matches the overlay diamonds).
    oc <- orchaRd::orchard_plot(h, xlab = xlab, flip = TRUE, k.pos = "left",
                                point.size = c(0.8, 10), trunk.size = 1)

    # Recolour the raw points (layer 1) by data source — as in hedges_comparison_plot.
    oc$layers[[1]]$mapping$colour <- ggplot2::aes(colour = real_source)$colour
    oc$layers[[1]]$mapping$fill   <- ggplot2::aes(fill = real_source)$fill

    # Corrected estimates to overlay on the single uncorrected trunk.
    yang <- corrected_est(rob_model)       # Bias-Robust   (Yang 2024)
    naka <- corrected_est(corr_res$model)  # Bias-Corrected (Nakagawa 2022)

    # Common effect-size range (falls back to this panel's data if not supplied).
    if (is.null(xlim)) {
        rng <- range(c(h$data$yi, yang$ci.lb, yang$ci.ub, naka$ci.lb, naka$ci.ub),
                     na.rm = TRUE, finite = TRUE)
        pad <- diff(rng) * 0.08
        xlim <- c(rng[1] - pad, rng[2] + pad)
    }
    txt_y <- xlim[2]  # right edge (effect-size axis is horizontal after flip)

    oc <- oc +
        # Bias-Robust (Yang) — nudged below the trunk. Black CI bar; coloured
        # diamond with a black border so it stands out against the bubbles.
        ggplot2::geom_linerange(data = yang,
            ggplot2::aes(x = name, ymin = ci.lb, ymax = ci.ub),
            colour = "black", linewidth = 1.2,
            position = ggplot2::position_nudge(x = -0.08), inherit.aes = FALSE) +
        ggplot2::geom_point(data = yang,
            ggplot2::aes(x = name, y = pred),
            fill = yang_col, colour = "black", shape = 23, size = 4, stroke = 0.9,
            position = ggplot2::position_nudge(x = -0.08), inherit.aes = FALSE) +
        # Bias-Corrected (Nakagawa) — nudged above the trunk. Black CI bar;
        # coloured diamond with a black border.
        ggplot2::geom_linerange(data = naka,
            ggplot2::aes(x = name, ymin = ci.lb, ymax = ci.ub),
            colour = "black", linewidth = 1.2,
            position = ggplot2::position_nudge(x = 0.08), inherit.aes = FALSE) +
        ggplot2::geom_point(data = naka,
            ggplot2::aes(x = name, y = pred),
            fill = naka_col, colour = "black", shape = 23, size = 4, stroke = 0.9,
            position = ggplot2::position_nudge(x = 0.08), inherit.aes = FALSE) +
        # Estimate text, right-aligned to the right edge so it stays in the box.
        # x positions mirror each diamond's nudge: Yang below (x<1), Naka above (x>1).
        ggplot2::annotate("text", x = 0.66, y = txt_y, hjust = 1, vjust = 0.5,
            colour = yang_col, size = 3.4, fontface = "bold",
            label = sprintf("Bias-Robust (Yang): %.2f [%.2f, %.2f]",
                            yang$pred, yang$ci.lb, yang$ci.ub)) +
        ggplot2::annotate("text", x = 1.34, y = txt_y, hjust = 1, vjust = 0.5,
            colour = naka_col, size = 3.4, fontface = "bold",
            label = sprintf("Bias-Corrected (Nakagawa): %.2f [%.2f, %.2f]",
                            naka$pred, naka$ci.lb, naka$ci.ub)) +
        ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(values = source_palette, name = "Data Source") +
        ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = source_palette, name = "Data Source") +
        ggplot2::ggtitle(title) +
        ggplot2::coord_flip(ylim = xlim) +
        ggplot2::theme(
            legend.position      = c(0.98, 0.02),
            legend.justification = c("right", "bottom"),
            legend.background    = ggplot2::element_rect(fill = scales::alpha("white", 0.75), color = "grey90"),
            legend.box           = "vertical",
            legend.margin        = ggplot2::margin(t = 4, r = 4, b = 4, l = 4),
            legend.title         = ggplot2::element_text(size = 9, face = "bold"),
            legend.text          = ggplot2::element_text(size = 8),
            plot.title           = ggplot2::element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
            # Panel identifier (a)/(b)/(c) in the top-left corner of the box.
            plot.tag             = ggplot2::element_text(size = 13, face = "bold", hjust = 0, vjust = 1),
            plot.tag.position    = c(0.015, 0.985)
        )

    oc
}

# Shared effect-size range across the three panels of a stack, padded for the
# right-aligned estimate text. Includes raw points + all corrected-estimate CIs.
compute_stack_xlim <- function(db, corr_models, rob_models) {
    ci_vals <- function(m) if (is.null(m)) numeric(0) else c(m$ci.lb[1], m$ci.ub[1])
    vals <- c(db$yi,
              unlist(lapply(corr_models, function(r) if (is.null(r)) numeric(0) else ci_vals(r$model))),
              unlist(lapply(rob_models, ci_vals)))
    vals <- vals[is.finite(vals)]
    rng <- range(vals)
    pad <- diff(rng) * 0.08
    c(rng[1] - pad, rng[2] + pad)
}

# Per-point source vectors for the Combined trunks (new rows then original rows,
# matching bind_rows() order — same assumption as hedges_comparison_plot).
n_h_new  <- nrow(orchaRd::mod_results(res_hedges_new$model,  group = "identifierStudyId")$data)
n_h_orig <- nrow(orchaRd::mod_results(res_hedges_orig$model, group = "identifierStudyId")$data)
src_h_comb <- c(rep("New Extraction (Santos)", n_h_new), rep("Original (Davies 2020)", n_h_orig))

n_o_new  <- nrow(orchaRd::mod_results(res_or_new$model,  group = "identifierStudyId")$data)
n_o_orig <- nrow(orchaRd::mod_results(res_or_orig$model, group = "identifierStudyId")$data)
src_o_comb <- c(rep("New Extraction (Santos)", n_o_new), rep("Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)", n_o_orig))



Hedges’ \(g\) (Continuous outcomes)


Show R code
# Shared x-axis range so the three Hedges panels align for easy comparison.
hedges_xlim <- compute_stack_xlim(
    db_hedges,
    list(res_hedges_new_corr, res_hedges_orig_corr, res_hedges_comb_corr),
    list(rob_hedges_new, rob_hedges_orig, rob_hedges_comb)
)

ph_new  <- make_pub_bias_subplot(res_hedges_new,  res_hedges_new_corr,  rob_hedges_new,  "New Extraction (Santos)", "Hedges' g", "New Extraction", xlim = hedges_xlim)
ph_orig <- make_pub_bias_subplot(res_hedges_orig, res_hedges_orig_corr, rob_hedges_orig, "Original (Davies 2020)",  "Hedges' g", "Original",       xlim = hedges_xlim)
ph_comb <- make_pub_bias_subplot(res_hedges_comb, res_hedges_comb_corr, rob_hedges_comb, src_h_comb,                "Hedges' g", "Combined",       xlim = hedges_xlim)

fig_hedges <- (ph_orig / ph_new / ph_comb) +
    patchwork::plot_annotation(
        tag_levels = "a", tag_prefix = "(", tag_suffix = ")",
        title = "Hedges' g — Uncorrected mean with Bias-Corrected & Bias-Robust overlays",
        subtitle = "Overlays: purple = Bias-Corrected (Nakagawa 2022), green = Bias-Robust (Yang 2024).",
        theme = ggplot2::theme(
            plot.title    = ggplot2::element_text(size = 15, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
            plot.subtitle = ggplot2::element_text(size = 11, face = "italic", hjust = 0.5)
        )
    )

print(fig_hedges)











Odds Ratio (Binary outcomes)


Show R code
# Shared x-axis range so the three OR panels align for easy comparison.
or_xlim <- compute_stack_xlim(
    db_or,
    list(res_or_new_corr, res_or_orig_corr, res_or_comb_corr),
    list(rob_or_new, rob_or_orig, rob_or_comb)
)

po_new  <- make_pub_bias_subplot(res_or_new,  res_or_new_corr,  rob_or_new,  "New Extraction (Santos)",       "Log Odds Ratio (lnOR)", "New Extraction", xlim = or_xlim)
po_orig <- make_pub_bias_subplot(res_or_orig, res_or_orig_corr, rob_or_orig, "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)", "Log Odds Ratio (lnOR)", "Original",       xlim = or_xlim)
po_comb <- make_pub_bias_subplot(res_or_comb, res_or_comb_corr, rob_or_comb, src_o_comb,                      "Log Odds Ratio (lnOR)", "Combined",       xlim = or_xlim)

fig_or <- (po_orig / po_new / po_comb) +
    patchwork::plot_annotation(
        tag_levels = "a", tag_prefix = "(", tag_suffix = ")",
        title = "Log Odds Ratio — Uncorrected mean with Bias-Corrected & Bias-Robust overlays",
        subtitle = "Overlays: purple = Bias-Corrected (Nakagawa 2022), green = Bias-Robust (Yang 2024).",
        theme = ggplot2::theme(
            plot.title    = ggplot2::element_text(size = 15, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),

    )

crumbs: 3  Overall Effect Size Calculation & Synthesis

title: Overall Effect Size Calculation & Synthesis
text:
Taxonomic Moderator (Heteroscedastic Model)
The combined synthesis pools effect sizes across very different organisms — insects and other arthropods, ray-finned fishes, and the remaining vertebrates (birds and mammals). Two questions follow naturally: does the average strength of mate-choice copying differ among these groups, and is the amount of heterogeneity itself group-specific? To answer both we fit a single rma.mv model with taxonomic group as a categorical moderator and a heteroscedastic random-effects structure that lets the variance components differ across groups.
Three design choices define this model:

Taxonomic grouping. Each species (full binomial) is assigned to Arthropods, Fish, or Other Vertebrates by genus. The genus uniquely determines the class for every species in these data, so the mapping is unambiguous (verified in the table below).
No phylogenetic term. The heterogeneity decomposition shows the phylogenetic component contributes very little variance, so it is dropped here; the species random intercept (taxonomySpecies) still absorbs among-species differences within each group.
Heteroscedasticity (struct = "HCS", rho = 0). The residual (within-study) effect-size variance is allowed to take a separate value in each taxonomic group, over a single pooled between-study variance, rather than forcing one common residual variance. This follows the orchaRd 2.0 recommendation for meta-regression with heterogeneous variances (Nakagawa et al. 2023): the heteroscedastic random term is written as an ~ moderator | unit formula (~ tax_group | identifierEffectSizeID) with a heteroscedastic compound-symmetry structure and the between-level covariance fixed to zero (rho = 0), since each effect size belongs to exactly one taxonomic group and a cross-group covariance is not identifiable. (A fuller structure that also made the between-study variance group-specific was considered but dropped: its per-group between-study variances collapse to zero for the groups with few studies, so it is over-parameterised for these data.)
Marginalised means. The moderator is fitted with an intercept (mods = ~ tax_group) and the per-group means, confidence intervals, and prediction intervals are obtained as marginalised means through orchaRd::mod_results() (which calls emmeans internally), again as recommended in the orchaRd 2.0 workflow. The overall test for differences among groups is the model’s omnibus moderator test (\(Q_M\)).



Show R code
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Genus-based taxonomic classification of the combined-dataset species.
# Classification keys on the FULL binomial (`species_phylo`); the genus alone
# unambiguously determines the class for every species in these datasets
# (cross-checked against the higher-taxon cascade in build_phylogeny.R).
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
arthropod_genera <- c("Drosophila", "Enchenopa", "Schizocosa", "Uca")
fish_genera <- c(
    "Amblyglyphidodon", "Brachyrhaphis", "Danio", "Etheostoma", "Gambusia",
    "Gasterosteus", "Limia", "Oryzias", "Poecilia", "Pomatoschistus",
    "Porichthys", "Rhabdoblennius", "Syngnathus"
)
othervert_genera <- c("Coturnix", "Dama", "Ficedula", "Molothrus", "Mus", "Rattus", "Taeniopygia")

tax_levels  <- c("Arthropods", "Fish", "Other Vertebrates")
tax_palette <- c("Arthropods" = "#E69F00", "Fish" = "#56B4E9", "Other Vertebrates" = "#009E73")

classify_taxon <- function(binomial) {
    genus <- stringr::word(as.character(binomial), 1)
    grp <- dplyr::case_when(
        genus %in% arthropod_genera ~ "Arthropods",
        genus %in% fish_genera      ~ "Fish",
        genus %in% othervert_genera ~ "Other Vertebrates",
        TRUE                        ~ NA_character_
    )
    factor(grp, levels = tax_levels)
}

# Add the taxonomic group to the combined Hedges' g and log OR datasets.
db_hedges_tax <- db_hedges %>% mutate(tax_group = classify_taxon(species_phylo))
db_or_tax     <- db_or     %>% mutate(tax_group = classify_taxon(species_phylo))

# Defensive check: every species should be classified.
unmapped_tax <- sort(unique(as.character(c(
    db_hedges_tax$species_phylo[is.na(db_hedges_tax$tax_group)],
    db_or_tax$species_phylo[is.na(db_or_tax$tax_group)]
))))
if (length(unmapped_tax) > 0) {
    warning("Unclassified species (no taxonomic group): ", paste(unmapped_tax, collapse = ", "))
}



Species-to-Group Mapping (verification)
The table below lists every species in the combined datasets, its full binomial, the species level used as a random effect (the epithet), and the taxonomic group it was assigned to, with the number of effect sizes it contributes to each effect-size class. This is the audit the analysis is built on — each row should look correct.








Model Fitting


Show R code
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Heteroscedastic taxonomic-moderator model, following the orchaRd 2.0
# heterogeneous-variance workflow (Nakagawa et al. 2023, vignette Example 4).
#
# * The moderator is fitted WITH an intercept (`mods = ~ tax_group`); per-group
#   means / CIs / PIs are obtained downstream as marginalised means via
#   orchaRd::mod_results() (emmeans), and the among-group test is the model's
#   omnibus moderator test (QM).
# * The heterogeneous (residual) variance is specified as a `~ tax_group | unit`
#   random term with struct = "HCS" and the between-level covariance fixed to
#   zero (rho = 0) — exactly the structure used in the orchaRd documentation.
#   With each unit belonging to a single group this estimates one residual
#   variance per group with no cross-group covariance.
# * PRIMARY structure: group-specific RESIDUAL variance
#   (`~ tax_group | identifierEffectSizeID`) over a single pooled between-study
#   variance (`~ 1 | identifierStudyId`) and a homoscedastic species intercept
#   (`~ 1 | taxonomySpecies`). NO phylogenetic term. (The fuller both-level
#   structure was dropped: its per-group between-study variances collapse to
#   zero for the thin groups, i.e. it is over-parameterised for these data.)
# * Each heteroscedastic structure is tried with several optimisers (nlminb,
#   optim/BFGS, optim/L-BFGS-B, then a high-iteration Nelder-Mead) before being
#   declared a failure; only if none converge does the cascade fall back to a
#   simpler structure. The vignette's default Nelder-Mead alone hits its
#   iteration cap on these data (convergence = 1), so robust optimisers go first.
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
fit_taxon_hetero_mv <- function(dat, group_var = "tax_group", yi = "yi", vi = "vi",
                                cluster_var = "identifierStudyId",
                                obs_var = "identifierEffectSizeID", rho = 0.5) {
    dat2 <- dat %>%
        filter(!is.na(.data[[yi]]), !is.na(.data[[vi]]), .data[[vi]] > 0, !is.na(.data[[group_var]]))
    if (nrow(dat2) == 0) {
        message("No complete cases found.")
        return(NULL)
    }
    dat2[[group_var]] <- droplevels(factor(dat2[[group_var]], levels = tax_levels))

    VCV <- metafor::vcalc(vi = dat2[[vi]], cluster = dat2[[cluster_var]], obs = dat2[[obs_var]], rho = rho, data = dat2)

    mods_f  <- stats::as.formula(paste0("~ ", group_var))                       # intercept-coded
    obs_f   <- stats::as.formula(paste0("~ ", group_var, " | ", obs_var))       # heteroscedastic residual

    # Optimisers tried (in order) for the heteroscedastic structures. The default
    # Nelder-Mead used in the orchaRd vignette hits its iteration cap on these
    # data (convergence = 1), so we lead with the more robust derivative-based
    # optimisers and only then fall back to a high-iteration Nelder-Mead.
    opt_grid <- list(
        "nlminb"            = list(optimizer = "nlminb", iter.max = 10000, eval.max = 10000),
        "optim/BFGS"        = list(optimizer = "optim", optmethod = "BFGS", maxit = 10000),
        "optim/L-BFGS-B"    = list(optimizer = "optim", optmethod = "L-BFGS-B", maxit = 10000),
        "optim/Nelder-Mead" = list(optimizer = "optim", optmethod = "Nelder-Mead", maxit = 50000, reltol = 1e-9)
    )

    # Build one rma.mv call for a candidate structure + optimiser control.
    build <- function(cand, ctrl) {
        args <- c(
            list(yi = dat2[[yi]], V = VCV, mods = mods_f, random = cand$random,
                 test = "t", dfs = "contain", method = "REML", sparse = TRUE, data = dat2),
            cand$args
        )
        if (!is.null(cand$struct)) args$struct <- cand$struct
        if (!is.null(ctrl))        args$control <- ctrl
        do.call(metafor::rma.mv, args)
    }

    # Candidate structures, tried in order. The cascade stops at the FIRST one
    # that fits without error; warnings do NOT stop the cascade but are recorded.
    # PRIMARY = residual-only HCS (orchaRd Example 4 default): a single pooled
    # between-study variance plus a group-specific residual variance. The fuller
    # both-level structure was dropped because its per-group between-study
    # variances collapse to the zero boundary for the thin groups (Other
    # Vertebrates, Arthropods) — i.e. it is over-parameterised for these data.
    candidates <- list(
        list(spec = "Heteroscedastic (residual only, per group; HCS, rho = 0)",
             random = list(~ 1 | identifierStudyId, obs_f, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies),
             struct = "HCS", args = list(rho = 0), het = TRUE),
        list(spec = "Homoscedastic (single variance per component)",
             random = list(~ 1 | identifierStudyId, ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies),
             struct = NULL, args = list(), het = FALSE)
    )

    # Attempt one candidate. Heteroscedastic structures cycle through opt_grid
    # until one converges; the homoscedastic structure uses the metafor default.
    # Errors AND warnings are captured (warnings do not abort).
    fit_once <- function(cand, ctrl) {
        warns <- character(0)
        t0 <- Sys.time()
        mod <- tryCatch(
            withCallingHandlers(
                build(cand, ctrl),
                warning = function(w) {
                    warns[[length(warns) + 1L]] <<- conditionMessage(w)
                    invokeRestart("muffleWarning")
                }
            ),
            error = function(e) structure(list(msg = conditionMessage(e)), class = "tax_fit_error")
        )
        list(mod = mod, warns = warns, secs = as.numeric(difftime(Sys.time(), t0, units = "secs")))
    }

    attempt <- function(cand) {
        if (!isTRUE(cand$het)) {
            r <- fit_once(cand, NULL)
            failed <- inherits(r$mod, "tax_fit_error")
            return(list(model = if (failed) NULL else r$mod, failed = failed,
                        error = if (failed) r$mod$msg else NA_character_,
                        warnings = r$warns, secs = r$secs, optimizer = "default (nlminb)"))
        }
        msgs <- character(0)
        total <- 0
        for (onm in names(opt_grid)) {
            r <- fit_once(cand, opt_grid[[onm]])
            total <- total + r$secs
            if (!inherits(r$mod, "tax_fit_error")) {
                return(list(model = r$mod, failed = FALSE, error = NA_character_,
                            warnings = r$warns, secs = total, optimizer = onm))
            }
            msgs <- c(msgs, paste0(onm, ": ", r$mod$msg))
        }
        list(model = NULL, failed = TRUE, error = paste(msgs, collapse = " || "),
             warnings = character(0), secs = total, optimizer = NA_character_)
    }

    diag_rows <- list()
    chosen <- NULL
    chosen_spec <- NA_character_
    for (i in seq_along(candidates)) {
        cand <- candidates[[i]]
        if (!is.null(chosen)) {
            diag_rows[[i]] <- tibble::tibble(
                stage = i, structure = cand$spec, status = "not attempted", optimizer = NA_character_,
                seconds = NA_real_, n_warnings = NA_integer_, detail = "an earlier structure succeeded"
            )
            next
        }
        a <- attempt(cand)
        status <- if (a$failed) "ERROR" else if (length(a$warnings) > 0) "OK (with warnings)" else "OK"
        detail <- if (a$failed) a$error else if (length(a$warnings) > 0) paste(a$warnings, collapse = " || ") else "clean fit"
        diag_rows[[i]] <- tibble::tibble(
            stage = i, structure = cand$spec, status = status, optimizer = a$optimizer,
            seconds = round(a$secs, 2), n_warnings = length(a$warnings), detail = detail
        )
        if (!a$failed) {
            chosen <- a$model
            chosen_spec <- cand$spec
        }
    }
    diagnostics <- dplyr::bind_rows(diag_rows)

    if (is.null(chosen)) {
        warning("All taxonomic-moderator structures failed to fit.")
        return(list(data = dat2, VCV = VCV, model = NULL, spec = "ALL FAILED", diagnostics = diagnostics))
    }
    list(data = dat2, VCV = VCV, model = chosen, spec = chosen_spec, diagnostics = diagnostics)
}

res_tax_hedges <- fit_taxon_hetero_mv(db_hedges_tax)
res_tax_or     <- fit_taxon_hetero_mv(db_or_tax)

# Omnibus among-group test. With the intercept-coded moderator, metafor's built-in
# QM tests all non-intercept coefficients jointly — i.e. exactly whether the group
# means differ — so it is used directly (no custom contrast matrix needed).
among_group_test <- function(res) {
    if (is.null(res) || is.null(res$model)) {
        return(NULL)
    }
    list(QM = res$model$QM, QMp = res$model$QMp, QMdf = res$model$QMdf)
}
qm_tax_hedges <- among_group_test(res_tax_hedges)
qm_tax_or     <- among_group_test(res_tax_or)

# Total I^2 (orchaRd if available; otherwise a Higgins typical-variance fallback
# so a heterogeneity figure is always reported even under the heteroscedastic
# structure).
total_i2 <- function(res) {
    if (is.null(res) || is.null(res$model)) {
        return(NA_real_)
    }
    m <- res$model
    i2 <- tryCatch(orchaRd::i2_ml(m), error = function(e) NULL)
    if (!is.null(i2) && any(grepl("Total", names(i2)))) {
        return(as.numeric(i2[grep("Total", names(i2))[1]]))
    }
    vi <- res$data$vi
    wi <- 1 / vi
    k  <- length(vi)
    s2 <- (k - 1) * sum(wi) / (sum(wi)^2 - sum(wi^2))
    vc <- sum(c(m$sigma2, m$tau2, m$gamma2), na.rm = TRUE)
    100 * vc / (vc + s2)
}
i2_tax_hedges <- total_i2(res_tax_hedges)
i2_tax_or     <- total_i2(res_tax_or)

# Persist the fitted results + cascade diagnostics so they can be inspected
# interactively in an R session WITHOUT re-rendering the book, e.g.:
#   b <- readRDS("outputs/1_effect_size_calculation_pipeline/taxon_model_fits.rds")
#   b$hedges$spec ; b$hedges$diagnostics ; summary(b$hedges$model)
# (also consumed by scripts/1_effect_size_calculation_pipeline/diagnose_taxon_model.R)
tryCatch({
    tax_out_dir <- here::here("outputs", "1_effect_size_calculation_pipeline")
    dir.create(tax_out_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
    saveRDS(
        list(
            hedges = res_tax_hedges, or = res_tax_or,
            qm = list(hedges = qm_tax_hedges, or = qm_tax_or),
            i2 = list(hedges = i2_tax_hedges, or = i2_tax_or),
            saved_at = Sys.time()
        ),
        file.path(tax_out_dir, "taxon_model_fits.rds")
    )
}, error = function(e) warning("Could not save taxon_model_fits.rds: ", conditionMessage(e)))




Fit Diagnostics
The model is fitted through a fallback cascade: the most flexible heteroscedastic structure is attempted first, and only if it errors does the next-simpler structure get tried (warnings do not trigger a fallback, but are captured and shown). Orchard Plot


Show R code
plot_taxon_orchard <- function(res, xlab, title) {
    if (is.null(res) || is.null(res$model)) {
        return(NULL)
    }
    mr <- orchaRd::mod_results(res$model, mod = "tax_group", group = "identifierStudyId")
    oc <- orchaRd::orchard_plot(mr, xlab = xlab, angle = 0, g = TRUE, k = TRUE)
    # Replace each group's k-count label with "estimate [95% CI]\nk = K (studies)"
    # using the same helper as the overall plots. tryCatch guards against any
    # orchaRd layer-order change so the figure still renders if it fails.
    oc <- tryCatch(add_ci_labels(oc, mr), error = function(e) {
        warning("add_ci_labels (taxon) failed: ", conditionMessage(e)); oc
    })
    oc +
        ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(values = tax_palette, name = "Taxonomic group", aesthetics = c("colour", "fill")) +
        ggplot2::ggtitle(title) +
        ggplot2::theme(
            plot.title = ggplot2::element_text(face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
            legend.position = "none"
        )
}

# Save a figure as a proper deliverable (PNG + PDF) in outputs/.
save_taxon_fig <- function(plot_obj, basename, width = 10, height = 9) {
    if (is.null(plot_obj)) {
        return(invisible())
    }
    tryCatch({
        d <- here::here("outputs", "1_effect_size_calculation_pipeline")
        dir.create(d, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
        ggplot2::ggsave(file.path(d, paste0(basename, ".png")), plot_obj, width = width, height = height, dpi = 300, bg = "white")
        ggplot2::ggsave(file.path(d, paste0(basename, ".pdf")), plot_obj, width = width, height = height)
    }, error = function(e) warning("Could not save ", basename, ": ", conditionMessage(e)))
}

p_tax_o <- plot_taxon_orchard(res_tax_or, "Log Odds Ratio (lnOR)", "Log OR by taxonomic group")

}
