title: Overall Effect Size Calculation & Synthesis
text:
Heterogeneity Decomposition (Partial \(I^2\))
Total heterogeneity (\(I^2_{\text{Total}}\)) quantifies the proportion of variance in the observed effect sizes that reflects true differences among effects rather than sampling error. In a multilevel model this total can be partitioned into the contribution of each random effect (Nakagawa et al. 2022). Here the variance is split among four components: between-study differences, the residual effect-size (within-study) level, a species (non-phylogenetic) component, and a phylogenetic component that captures variance shared among species in proportion to their relatedness. We compute these partial \(I^2\) values with orchaRd::i2_ml() for each uncorrected model.


Show R code
# Human-readable labels for the i2_ml components (names are prefixed with "I2_")
comp_labels <- c(
    Total                  = "Total",
    identifierStudyId      = "Study",
    identifierEffectSizeID = "Effect size (residual)",
    taxonomySpecies        = "Species (non-phylogenetic)",
    species_phylo          = "Phylogeny"
)

# Pull the partial I^2 vector from a fitted model into tidy long form
get_i2 <- function(res, effect, model) {
    if (is.null(res) || is.null(res$model)) {
        return(NULL)
    }
    v <- tryCatch(orchaRd::i2_ml(res$model), error = function(e) NULL)
    if (is.null(v)) {
        return(NULL)
    }
    tibble::tibble(
        Effect    = effect,
        Model     = model,
        Component = sub("^I2_", "", names(v)),
        I2        = as.numeric(v)
    )
}

i2_long <- dplyr::bind_rows(
    get_i2(res_hedges_new, "Hedges' g", "New Only"),
    get_i2(res_hedges_orig, "Hedges' g", "Original Only"),
    get_i2(res_hedges_comb, "Hedges' g", "Combined"),
    get_i2(res_or_new, "Log OR", "New Only"),
    get_i2(res_or_orig, "Log OR", "Original Only"),
    get_i2(res_or_comb, "Log OR", "Combined")
) %>%
    mutate(ComponentLabel = dplyr::recode(Component, !!!comp_labels))

# Lookup helper for inline narrative (returns NA if a component is absent)
i2get <- function(effect, model, component) {
    v <- i2_long$I2[i2_long$Effect == effect & i2_long$Model == model & i2_long$ComponentLabel == component]
    if (length(v) == 0 || all(is.na(v))) NA_real_ else v[1]
}



Partial \(I^2\) by Random Effect








Interpreting the Heterogeneity
The combined models show that mate-choice copying effect sizes are highly heterogeneous: total \(I^2\) reaches 85.7% for Hedges’ \(g\) and 74.7% for the log odds ratio. In other words, the large majority of the observed variation reflects genuine differences among effects rather than sampling error alone, so the pooled means should be read as averages over a broad distribution of true effects rather than as a single common effect.
Decomposing that heterogeneity is where the phylogeny becomes informative. For the combined Hedges’ \(g\) model, the phylogenetic component accounts for 3.5% of the variance, the non-phylogenetic species component for 3.1%, between-study differences for 7.4%, and the residual effect-size level for 71.7%. The combined log-odds-ratio model partitions similarly: phylogeny 1.1%, species 23.9%, study 4.3%, and residual 45.4%.
The size of the phylogenetic component is the key new result. A substantial value would indicate that closely related species tend to show similar degrees of mate-choice copying — i.e., the trait carries a phylogenetic signal and effects are not independent across the tree — which would caution against treating species as exchangeable and would motivate phylogenetically informed interpretation. A small phylogenetic component, by contrast, indicates that relatedness explains little of the among-species variation: most of the species-level differences are idiosyncratic (captured by the non-phylogenetic species term) rather than inherited, and the earlier non-phylogenetic models were not materially biased by ignoring the tree. The contrast between the phylogenetic and non-phylogenetic species components is therefore the most direct readout of how much shared ancestry, as opposed to species-specific biology, structures the data.
Two further patterns are worth noting. First, the residual effect-size level typically absorbs a large share of the variance, reflecting heterogeneity among individual trials within studies (different paradigms, demonstrators, and conditions). Second, comparing the New-Only, Original-Only, and Combined columns shows whether the new extraction introduces or dilutes heterogeneity relative to the legacy datasets; where the new and original subsets allocate variance differently, the combined estimate reflects a weighted blend of the two. Because this high heterogeneity is the rule rather than the exception, it justifies the moderator and sub-group analyses pursued elsewhere in the workflow, and it is the reason the pooled estimates are reported with prediction intervals in addition to confidence intervals.

title: Overall Effect Size Calculation & Synthesis
section: Sensitivity Analysis: Impact of the VCV Structure
text:
Sensitivity Analysis: Impact of the VCV Structure
To evaluate the robustness of our meta-analytic results, we conduct a sensitivity analysis comparing the main multilevel models (which incorporate a Variance-Covariance [VCV] matrix with an assumed within-study correlation \(\rho = 0.5\)) against simplified multilevel models that ignore this correlation (i.e., treating effect size variances as independent diagonal elements, \(V = v_i\)).
Ignoring within-study correlation can lead to improper weighting of studies with multiple effect sizes and can bias the standard errors and confidence intervals of the pooled estimates.


Show R code
# 1. Define comparison function
compare_vcv_models <- function(dat, name, yi = "yi", vi = "vi", cluster_var = "identifierStudyId", obs_var = "identifierEffectSizeID", rho = 0.5) {
    dat2 <- dat %>%
        filter(!is.na(.data[[yi]]), !is.na(.data[[vi]]), .data[[vi]] > 0)

    if (nrow(dat2) == 0) {
        return(NULL)
    }

    # A. With VCV (rho = 0.5)
    VCV <- metafor::vcalc(
        vi = dat2[[vi]],
        cluster = dat2[[cluster_var]],
        obs = dat2[[obs_var]],
        rho = rho,
        data = dat2
    )
    mod_w_vcv <- metafor::rma.mv(
        yi = dat2[[yi]],
        V = VCV,
        random = list(
            ~ 1 | identifierStudyId,
            ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID,
            ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies
        ),
        test = "t",
        method = "REML",
        sparse = TRUE,
        data = dat2
    )

    # B. Without VCV (diagonal V = vi)
    mod_no_vcv <- metafor::rma.mv(
        yi = dat2[[yi]],
        V = dat2[[vi]],
        random = list(
            ~ 1 | identifierStudyId,
            ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID,
            ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies
        ),
        test = "t",
        method = "REML",
        sparse = TRUE,
        data = dat2
    )

    # Combine results
    data.frame(
        Analysis = name,
        Model_Type = c("With VCV (rho = 0.5)", "Without VCV (Independent)"),
        k = c(mod_w_vcv$k, mod_no_vcv$k),
        Estimate = c(mod_w_vcv$b[1], mod_no_vcv$b[1]),
        SE = c(mod_w_vcv$se[1], mod_no_vcv$se[1]),
        CI_Lower = c(mod_w_vcv$ci.lb[1], mod_no_vcv$ci.lb[1]),
        CI_Upper = c(mod_w_vcv$ci.ub[1], mod_no_vcv$ci.ub[1]),
        p_val = c(mod_w_vcv$pval[1], mod_no_vcv$pval[1])
    )
}

# 2. Run comparisons across all subsets
comp_hedges_new <- compare_vcv_models(db_hedges %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "New Extraction (Santos)"), "Hedges' g (New Only)")
comp_hedges_orig <- compare_vcv_models(db_hedges %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "Original (Davies 2020)"), "Hedges' g (Original Only)")
comp_hedges_comb <- compare_vcv_models(db_hedges, "Hedges' g (Combined)")

comp_or_new <- compare_vcv_models(db_or %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "New Extraction (Santos)"), "Log OR (New Only)")
comp_or_orig <- compare_vcv_models(db_or %>% filter(Dataset_Source == "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)"), "Log OR (Original Only)")
comp_or_comb <- compare_vcv_models(db_or, "Log OR (Combined)")

# 3. Bind all comparisons together
vcv_comparison_results <- bind_rows(
    comp_hedges_new, comp_hedges_orig, comp_hedges_comb,
    comp_or_new, comp_or_orig, comp_or_comb
)



Sensitivity Comparison Table
The interactive table below compares the pool estimate, standard error (SE), 95% Confidence Intervals, and p-values for both models across all subsets.








Key Methodological Findings

Marginal Shifts in Pooled Estimates: Treating effect sizes as completely independent (Without VCV) causes very minor changes to the overall pooled estimates. For the combined dataset, the Hedges’ \(g\) estimate shifts from \(0.46\) to \(0.48\), while the Log Odds Ratio (lnOR) estimate shifts from \(0.60\) to \(0.64\). This indicates that the main directional conclusions of the meta-analyses are robust to VCV specification.
Standard Error Shifts: In this multilevel model specification (which partitions variance using nested random intercepts), omitting the VCV matrix and assuming independence leads to a slight inflation of the standard error and wider confidence intervals for the combined analyses:

Hedges’ \(g\) (Combined): SE increases from \(0.07\) to \(0.07\) (CI width increases from \(0.29\) to \(0.30\)).
Log Odds Ratio (Combined): SE increases from \(0.14\) to \(0.15\) (CI width increases from \(0.54\) to \(0.59\)).

This demonstrates that accounting for known within-study covariance via vcalc yields more precise, correctly-weighted estimates.
Impact on Statistical Significance: For continuous outcomes (Hedges’ \(g\) Combined), the pooled estimate is statistically significant with the VCV matrix (\(p < 0.0001\)) and statistically significant without it (\(p < 0.0001\)). Binary outcomes (Log OR Combined) are statistically significant under both specifications (\(p < 0.0001\) with VCV; \(p < 0.0001\) without).

Therefore, we conclude that while the overall direction and magnitude of the estimates are stable, incorporating the VCV matrix ensures appropriate weighting, controlling for within-study correlation, and obtaining accurate standard errors and statistical tests.

title: Publication Bias Diagnostics
text:
Introduction & Methodology
Publication bias is a critical threat to the validity of meta-analytic syntheses. If studies with statistically non-significant or small effects are systematically omitted from the literature, the pooled meta-analytic summary will be biased upward.
To thoroughly evaluate the presence of publication bias and report-level selection in our Mate-Choice Copying (MCC) dataset, we employ three complementary diagnostic approaches, keeping the exact multilevel modeling logic (e.g., nested random intercepts and taxonomy control) used in our main overall effect calculations:

title: Publication Bias Diagnostics
text:
Visual Inspection (Funnel Plots): We plot effect size estimates against their precision (inverse of the standard error, \(1/\text{SE}\)) to check for visual asymmetry around the overall mean estimate.
Egger’s Meta-Regression (Small-Study Effects): We test whether study precision significantly moderates effect size. Following the recommendations of Nakagawa et al. (2022) and Stanley (2012, 2014), we implement a two-step approach:

We first fit the Egger regression with the standard error (\(\text{SE} = \sqrt{v_i}\)) as the moderator. The slope tests for small-study effects, and the intercept estimates the effect that a perfectly precise study (\(\text{SE} = 0\)) would report.
When that intercept is statistically significant — i.e., a genuine non-zero effect is likely present — the standard-error version is known to under-estimate the true effect. We therefore re-fit the regression with the sampling variance (\(\text{V} = v_i\)) as the moderator, which gives a less biased estimate of the bias-adjusted overall mean.

Decline Effect / Time-Lag Bias: We test whether effect sizes shrink over time by adding publication year (mean-centered) to our models while simultaneously controlling for study precision.
Bias-Corrected Meta-Analytic Means (Method Comparison): Finally, we estimate the adjusted overall mean under two complementary correction frameworks and compare them directly. The current approach follows Nakagawa et al. (2022): the intercept of a multilevel meta-regression on the sampling variance (\(v_i\)) is interpreted as the mean when sampling error is zero (i.e., an infinitely precise study). The new approach follows Yang et al. (2024): a bias-robust weighting scheme (a fixed-/common-effect GLS estimator that is less susceptible to selective reporting) is combined with cluster-robust (CR2) variance estimation to handle the non-independence of effect sizes within studies. We overlay both corrected means on an orchard plot using orchaRd::pub_bias_plot().

title: Publication Bias Diagnostics
text:
Data Loading and Alignment
We read, clean, and align the new extractions and original datasets, extracting sample sizes and publication years to construct our precision and time-lag moderators.


Show R code
# Paths to datasets
path_new <- here::here("data", "1_effect_size_calculation_pipeline", "Data Extraction Mate Choice Meta Analysis.xlsx")
path_davies <- here::here("data", "original_meta_analyses_datasets", "Davies_et_al_2020_Final_data.xlsx")
path_jones <- here::here("data", "original_meta_analyses_datasets", "Jones_DuVal_2019_data.CSV")

# A. Read raw spreadsheets
db_new_raw <- readxl::read_excel(path_new, sheet = "EduardoExtraction")
db_davies_raw <- readxl::read_excel(path_davies, sheet = "Data")
db_jones_raw <- readr::read_csv(path_jones)

# B. Clean new extractions
db_new <- db_new_raw %>%
    filter(is.na(RemoveFromDataset) | RemoveFromDataset != "Yes") %>%
    mutate(
        effectSizeCalculatedHedgesD = as.numeric(effectSizeCalculatedHedgesD),
        effectSizeCalculatedHedgesDVariance = as.numeric(effectSizeCalculatedHedgesDVariance),
        effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatio = as.numeric(effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatio),
        effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatioVariance = as.numeric(effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatioVariance),
        logOddsRatio = log(effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatio),
        logOddsRatioVariance = effectSizeCalculatedOddsRatioVariance,
        identifierStudyYear = as.numeric(identifierStudyYear),
        controlN = as.numeric(controlN),
        treatmentN = as.numeric(treatmentN),
        effectSizesampleSize = as.numeric(effectSizesampleSize)
    )

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Hedges' g Dataset Alignment (Continuous)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
hedges_new <- db_new %>%
    filter(!is.na(effectSizeCalculatedHedgesD), !is.na(effectSizeCalculatedHedgesDVariance)) %>%
    select(
        yi = effectSizeCalculatedHedgesD,
        vi = effectSizeCalculatedHedgesDVariance,
        identifierStudyId,
        identifierEffectSizeID,
        taxonomySpecies,
        Year = identifierStudyYear,
        controlN,
        treatmentN,
        totalN = effectSizesampleSize
    ) %>%
    mutate(
        identifierStudyId = as.character(identifierStudyId),
        identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(identifierEffectSizeID),
        taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(word(as.character(taxonomySpecies), -1)),
        Dataset_Source = "New Extraction (Santos)"
    )

hedges_davies <- db_davies_raw %>%
    mutate(
        Hedges_d_directional = as.numeric(Hedges_d_directional),
        Variance = as.numeric(Variance),
        Year = as.numeric(Year),
        N_control = as.numeric(N_control),
        N_treatment = as.numeric(N_treatment),
        Total_n_analysis = as.numeric(Total_n_analysis)
    ) %>%
    filter(!is.na(Hedges_d_directional), !is.na(Variance), Variance > 0) %>%
    select(
        yi = Hedges_d_directional,
        vi = Variance,
        identifierStudyId = Author,
        identifierEffectSizeID = Effect_size_no,
        taxonomySpecies = Species_latin,
        Year,
        controlN = N_control,
        treatmentN = N_treatment,
        totalN = Total_n_analysis
    ) %>%
    mutate(
        identifierStudyId = as.character(identifierStudyId),
        identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(identifierEffectSizeID),
        taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(word(as.character(taxonomySpecies), -1)),
        Dataset_Source = "Original (Davies 2020)"
    )

db_hedges <- bind_rows(hedges_new, hedges_davies) %>%
    mutate(across(c(identifierStudyId, taxonomySpecies, Dataset_Source), as.factor))

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. Log Odds Ratio Dataset Alignment (Binary)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
or_new <- db_new %>%
    filter(!is.na(logOddsRatio), !is.na(logOddsRatioVariance)) %>%
    select(
        yi = logOddsRatio,
        vi = logOddsRatioVariance,
        identifierStudyId,
        identifierEffectSizeID,
        taxonomySpecies,
        Year = identifierStudyYear
    ) %>%
    mutate(
        identifierStudyId = as.character(identifierStudyId),
        identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(identifierEffectSizeID),
        taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(word(as.character(taxonomySpecies), -1)),
        Dataset_Source = "New Extraction (Santos)"
    )

or_jones <- db_jones_raw %>%
    filter(!is.na(lnOR), !is.na(VlnOR), VlnOR > 0) %>%
    select(
        yi = lnOR,
        vi = VlnOR,
        identifierStudyId = study,
        identifierEffectSizeID = study_Exp,
        taxonomySpecies = Species,
        Year = year
    ) %>%
    mutate(
        identifierStudyId = as.character(identifierStudyId),
        identifierEffectSizeID = as.character(identifierEffectSizeID),
        taxonomySpecies = str_to_lower(as.character(taxonomySpecies)),
        Dataset_Source = "Original (Jones & DuVal 2019)"
    )

db_or <- bind_rows(or_new, or_jones) %>%
    mutate(across(c(identifierStudyId, taxonomySpecies, Dataset_Source), as.factor))

Diagnostics by Effect Size Class

Hedges’ \(g\) (Continuous outcomes)Odds Ratio (Binary outcomes)


We fit multilevel models on the continuous combined dataset (\(k = 227\)) to run our diagnostics.

1. Baseline Model & Funnel Plot
We run the intercept-only model to establish the global mean effect size and plot precision (\(1/\text{SE}\)) against the standardized residuals.


Show R code
# Intercept-only model
VCV_hedges <- metafor::vcalc(vi = vi, cluster = identifierStudyId, obs = identifierEffectSizeID, rho = 0.5, data = db_hedges)

ma_hedges <- metafor::rma.mv(
    yi = yi,
    V = VCV_hedges,
    random = list(~ 1 | identifierStudyId, ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies),
    test = "t",
    method = "REML",
    sparse = TRUE,
    data = db_hedges
)

summary(ma_hedges)

Show R code
# Extract BLUPs for random effects
blups <- ranef(ma_hedges)
study_ef <- blups$identifierStudyId$intrcpt[match(db_hedges$identifierStudyId, row.names(blups$identifierStudyId))]
obs_ef <- blups$identifierEffectSizeID$intrcpt[match(db_hedges$identifierEffectSizeID, row.names(blups$identifierEffectSizeID))]
spec_ef <- blups$taxonomySpecies$intrcpt[match(db_hedges$taxonomySpecies, row.names(blups$taxonomySpecies))]

# Handle any missing values by replacing with 0
study_ef[is.na(study_ef)] <- 0
obs_ef[is.na(obs_ef)] <- 0
spec_ef[is.na(spec_ef)] <- 0

# Marginal residuals: observed - fixed-effect prediction
resid_marg <- db_hedges$yi - predict(ma_hedges)[[1]]

# Conditional residuals: observed - fixed - random intercepts (study, obs, species)
resid_cond <- db_hedges$yi - (predict(ma_hedges)[[1]] + study_ef + obs_ef + spec_ef)

# Side-by-side funnel plots of marginal vs. conditional residuals,
# with (a)/(b) panel tags. Drawn on-screen and saved to a manuscript PDF.
draw_funnels(resid_marg, resid_cond, db_hedges$vi)









Show R code
save_base_pdf(
    "funnel_hedges",
    function() draw_funnels(resid_marg, resid_cond, db_hedges$vi)
)


quartz_off_screen 
                2 








NoteReading the funnel plots



Both panels plot each effect size against its precision (\(1/\text{SE}\)), but differ in what is removed before plotting:

(a) Marginal residuals are the deviations of each observed effect from the fixed-effect (intercept) prediction — the pooled mean — so they still carry all of the random-effects heterogeneity (between-study, between-effect-size, and between-species). Asymmetry here — a shortage of small, imprecise studies on one side of the funnel — is the classic visual signature of small-study and publication bias. Because genuine heterogeneity can mimic or exaggerate that asymmetry, apparent gaps in (a) should be read cautiously and confirmed against the formal Egger’s regression below.
(b) Conditional residuals additionally subtract the estimated random intercepts (study, effect-size, and species BLUPs), so the heterogeneity captured by the multilevel structure is partialled out. A symmetric, evenly-filled funnel here means that once the nesting is accounted for there is little precision-related asymmetry left — i.e., the asymmetry seen in (a) is largely explained by the modelled random effects rather than by selective reporting. Any asymmetry that persists in (b) is the more credible visual indication of true small-study effects.





2. Egger’s Meta-Regression (Small-Study Effects)
To perform Egger’s regression on Hedges’ \(g\), we follow the two-step framework recommended by Nakagawa et al. (2022) and Stanley (2012, 2014). We use the sampling standard error (\(\text{SE} = \sqrt{v_i}\)) as our precision proxy. If this moderator is statistically significant (\(p < 0.05\)), it indicates a significant small-study effect (larger standard errors / smaller sample sizes associated with larger effect magnitudes).
When the intercept of the standard-error model is significant (a genuine non-zero effect is likely present), that intercept tends to under-estimate the true effect. We therefore apply the second step: re-fit Egger’s regression with the sampling variance (\(\text{V} = \text{vi}\)) as the moderator, whose intercept gives a less biased estimate of the bias-adjusted overall mean.


Show R code
# Calculate standard error precision proxy
db_hedges_egger <- db_hedges %>%
    mutate(se = sqrt(vi)) %>%
    filter(!is.na(se))

# Re-estimate VCV on this subset
VCV_hedges_egger <- metafor::vcalc(vi = vi, cluster = identifierStudyId, obs = identifierEffectSizeID, rho = 0.5, data = db_hedges_egger)

# Step 1: Egger regression model with standard error as moderator
egger_hedges <- metafor::rma.mv(
    yi = yi,
    V = VCV_hedges_egger,
    mods = ~ 1 + se,
    random = list(~ 1 | identifierStudyId, ~ 1 | identifierEffectSizeID, ~ 1 | taxonomySpecies),
    test = "t",
    method = "REML",
    sparse = TRUE,
    data = db_hedges_egger
)

We visualize the Egger regression using a bubble plot. Effect sizes are plotted as circles scaled by precision, with the predicted regression line showing the relationship between sample size and effect size.

p_egger_hedges <- orchaRd::bubble_plot(
    egger_hedges,
    mod = "se",
    group = "identifierStudyId",
    xlab = "Standard Error of Hedges' g (se)",
    ylab = "Effect size (Hedges' g)"
)

# Color points by real source
p_egger_hedges$layers[[1]]$data$real_source <- db_hedges_egger$Dataset_Source
p_egger_hedges$layers[[1]]$mapping$colour <- ggplot2::aes(colour = real_source)$colour
p_egger_hedges$layers[[1]]$mapping$fill <- ggplot2::aes(fill = real_source)$fill

p_egger_hedges <- p_egger_hedges +
    ggplot2::scale_colour_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Davies 2020)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") +
    ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = c("New Extraction (Santos)" = "#D55E00", "Original (Davies 2020)" = "#0072B2"), name = "Data Source") +
    ggplot2::ggtitle("Egger's Meta-Regression (Hedges' g)") +
    ggplot2::theme_minimal()

print(p_egger_hedges)










[!NOTE] The slope for standard error (se) is positive (\(\beta = 4.36\), \(SE = 0.58\), \(t = 7.56\), \(p < 0.0001\)), indicating clear evidence of small-study effects.
Two-Step Correction: The intercept of the standard error model is statistically significant (\(p < 0.0001\)). Following the two-step recommendation of Nakagawa et al. (2022) and Stanley (2012, 2014), we re-fit Egger’s regression using the sampling variance (vi) as the moderator. In this corrected model, the adjusted overall meta-analytic mean is estimated at \(\beta_0 = -0.21\) (\(SE = 0.10\), \(t = -2.11\), \(p = 0.036\)).

draw_funnels(resid_marg, resid_cond, db_or$vi)

quartz_off_screen 
                2 








NoteReading the funnel plots



Both panels plot each \(\ln\text{OR}\) effect size against its precision (\(1/\text{SE}\)), but differ in what is removed before plotting:

(a) Marginal residuals are the deviations of each observed effect from the fixed-effect (intercept) prediction — the pooled mean — so they still carry all of the random-effects heterogeneity (between-study, between-effect-size, and between-species). Asymmetry here — a shortage of small, imprecise studies on one side of the funnel — is the classic visual signature of small-study and publication bias. Because genuine heterogeneity can mimic or exaggerate that asymmetry, apparent gaps in (a) should be read cautiously and confirmed against the formal Egger’s regression below.
(b) Conditional residuals additionally subtract the estimated random intercepts (study, effect-size, and species BLUPs), so the heterogeneity captured by the multilevel structure is partialled out. A symmetric, evenly-filled funnel here means that once the nesting is accounted for there is little precision-related asymmetry left — i.e., the asymmetry seen in (a) is largely explained by the modelled random effects rather than by selective reporting. Any asymmetry that persists in (b) is the more credible visual indication of true small-study effects.





2. Egger’s Meta-Regression (Small-Study Effects)
For Odds Ratio studies, we use the standard error (\(\text{SE} = \sqrt{v_i}\)) as our precision proxy in the two-step framework. If the standard error moderator slope is statistically significant (\(p < 0.05\)), it represents a significant small-study effect.
As with the continuous outcomes, we apply the second step when the intercept of the standard-error model is significant: we re-fit Egger’s regression with the sampling variance (\(\text{V} = \text{vi}\)) as the moderator, whose intercept gives a less biased estimate of the bias-adjusted overall mean.

)

title: Publication Bias Diagnostics
section: Bias-Corrected Meta-Analytic Means: Current vs. New Method
text:
Bias-Corrected Meta-Analytic Means: Current vs. New Method
The diagnostics above detect small-study and time-lag bias. Here we turn to correcting the pooled mean, and we directly compare two correction philosophies on the same data.

Current method — Nakagawa et al. (2022) regression correction. We re-use the multilevel meta-regression that includes the sampling variance (\(v_i\)) as a moderator (the Step-2 models egger_hedges_v / egger_or_v fitted above). Its intercept estimates the meta-analytic mean extrapolated to \(v_i = 0\) — i.e., the effect a hypothetical infinitely-precise (infinite-\(n\)) study would report, with the small-study component removed. This is the “bias-corrected estimate” in Yang et al. (2024)’s terminology and in orchaRd.
New method — Yang et al. (2024) bias-robust estimation. A two-step procedure: (1) fit a fixed-/common-effect model, whose precision-based GLS weighting is far less inflated by selectively-reported small studies than the random-effects mean; then (2) apply cluster-robust (CR2) variance estimation (clubSandwich), clustering by study, so that the standard errors and confidence intervals remain valid under the non-independence of multiple effect sizes per study. This is the “bias-robust estimate”.

We compute both, overlay them on an orchard plot of the uncorrected multilevel mean with orchaRd::pub_bias_plot(), and tabulate the three estimates (uncorrected vs. the two corrections) side by side.

Hedges’ \(g\) (Continuous outcomes)Odds Ratio (Binary outcomes)




Show R code
# --- New method: Yang et al. (2024) bias-robust estimate ---------------------
# Step 1: common-/fixed-effect model (bias-robust GLS weighting).
fe_hedges <- metafor::rma(
    yi = yi,
    vi = vi,
    data = db_hedges_egger,
    method = "FE",
    test = "t"
)

# Step 2: cluster-robust (CR2) variance estimation, clustering by study,
# to account for non-independence of multiple effect sizes per study.
fe_hedges_robust <- metafor::robust(
    fe_hedges,
    cluster = db_hedges_egger$identifierStudyId,
    clubSandwich = TRUE
)

summary(fe_hedges_robust)



Fixed-Effects Model (k = 227)

   logLik   deviance        AIC        BIC       AICc   
-499.4348  1119.8276  1000.8695  1004.2945  1000.8873   

I^2 (total heterogeneity / total variability):   79.82%
H^2 (total variability / sampling variability):  4.95

Test for Heterogeneity:
Q(df = 226) = 1119.8276, p-val < .0001

Number of estimates:   227
Number of clusters:    80
Estimates per cluster: 1-12 (mean: 2.84, median: 2)

Model Results:

estimate      se¹    tval¹     df¹    pval¹   ci.lb¹   ci.ub¹      
  0.3445  0.0490   7.0335   32.83   <.0001   0.2449   0.4442   *** 

---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

1) results based on cluster-robust inference (var-cov estimator: CR2,
   approx t-test and confidence interval, df: Satterthwaite approx)


Show R code
# `egger_hedges_v` (fitted above) is the current-method Nakagawa et al. (2022)
# vi-moderator model; its intercept is the bias-corrected mean at vi = 0.




Show R code
# Orchard plot of the uncorrected multilevel meta-analytic mean ...
oc_hedges <- orchaRd::orchard_plot(
    ma_hedges,
    group = "identifierStudyId",
    xlab = "Hedges' g",
    angle = 0
)

# ... with both corrected means overlaid:
#   red  = Yang et al. 2024 "Bias Robust"     (fixed-effect + CR2 robust)
#   blue = Nakagawa et al. 2022 "Bias Corrected" (vi-moderator intercept)
p_pubbias_hedges <- orchaRd::pub_bias_plot(
    oc_hedges,
    fe_model = fe_hedges_robust,
    v_model  = egger_hedges_v
) +
    ggplot2::ggtitle("Bias-Corrected Means — Hedges' g") +
    ggplot2::theme_minimal()

print(p_pubbias_hedges)










[!NOTE] The uncorrected multilevel mean is \(\hat\mu = 0.46\) (95% CI [0.31, 0.60]). The current Nakagawa correction gives a bias-corrected mean of \(-0.21\) (95% CI [-0.41, -0.01]), and the new Yang bias-robust estimate is \(0.34\) (95% CI [0.24, 0.44]).





Show R code
# --- New method: Yang et al. (2024) bias-robust estimate ---------------------
fe_or <- metafor::rma(
    yi = yi,
    vi = vi,
    data = db_or_egger,
    method = "FE",
    test = "t"
)

fe_or_robust <- metafor::robust(
    fe_or,
    cluster = db_or_egger$identifierStudyId,
    clubSandwich = TRUE
)

summary(fe_or_robust)



Fixed-Effects Model (k = 172)

# vi-moderator model; its intercept is the bias-corrected mean at vi = 0.




Show R code

    ma_or,
    group = "identifierStudyId",
    xlab = "Log Odds Ratio (lnOR)",
    angle = 0
)

    oc_or,
    fe_model = fe_or_robust,
    v_model  = egger_or_v
) +
    ggplot2::ggtitle("Bias-Corrected Means — Log Odds Ratio") +
    ggplot2::theme_minimal()

print(p_pubbias_or)










[!NOTE] The uncorrected multilevel mean is \(\hat\mu = 0.60\) (95% CI [0.33, 0.87]) on the \(\ln\text{OR}\) scale. The current Nakagawa correction gives \(0.10\) (95% CI [-0.22, 0.42]), and the new Yang bias-robust estimate is \(0.57\) (95% CI [0.42, 0.73]).





Side-by-Side Comparison Table







[!IMPORTANT] How to read the comparison. Both corrections should pull the pooled estimate toward zero relative to the uncorrected multilevel mean if small-study/selective-reporting bias is present; the Pct_Change_vs_Uncorrected column quantifies that shrinkage. The two methods answer slightly different questions. The Nakagawa intercept is a model-based extrapolation to a perfectly precise study and tends to give wide confidence intervals (it is an out-of-range prediction). The Yang bias-robust estimate stays within the observed data, down-weights heterogeneity-driven inflation through fixed-effect weighting, and uses cluster-robust CR2 standard errors that explicitly respect the within-study dependence — making it a more conservative, inference-valid sensitivity analysis. Agreement between the two corrections strengthens confidence in the adjusted conclusion; divergence flags that the corrected mean is sensitive to modelling assumptions.

title: Publication Bias Diagnostics
text:
Methodological Synthesis & Discussion
We compile the results of the small-study effect regressions and time-lag meta-regressions for both effect size classes below:

Publication Bias Comparison Table








Main Takeaways

Small-Study Effects: The Egger’s regressions indicate statistically significant small-study effects for Hedges’ \(g\) (\(p < 0.0001\)) and statistically significant small-study effects for Log Odds Ratio (\(p < 0.0001\)). Where present, smaller and less precise trials systematically report larger mate-choice copying effect sizes—a classic signature of publication selection, in which small-sample studies are more likely to be published or reported if they find large, hypothesis-supportive effects.
Decline Effect (Time-Lag Bias) in Binary Outcomes: The publication-year trend for binary outcomes is statistically significant (\(\beta = -0.03\), \(p = 0.008\)). Over the last two decades, the reported strength of mate-choice copying (measured by Log Odds Ratios) has decreased, a pattern typical of fields experiencing a novelty effect in which early breakthrough publications report large effect sizes and subsequent rigorous replications reveal more moderate, realistic estimates.
Time Trend in Continuous Outcomes: For Hedges’ \(g\) (continuous), the publication-year trend is non-significant (\(\beta = 0.00\), \(p = 0.761\)). This suggests the continuous reporting scales have been relatively stable in magnitude over the history of the literature.
Bias-Corrected Means — Current vs. New Method: We compared two correction frameworks on the adjusted pooled mean. For Hedges’ \(g\), the current Nakagawa (2022) \(v_i\)-intercept correction yields \(-0.21\) while the new Yang (2024) bias-robust (fixed-effect + CR2) estimate yields \(0.34\) (uncorrected multilevel mean: \(0.46\)). For Log Odds Ratio, the corresponding values are \(0.10\) (Nakagawa) and \(0.57\) (Yang), against an uncorrected mean of \(0.60\). The Yang bias-robust estimate is generally the more conservative, inference-valid option because it respects within-study dependence through cluster-robust variance estimation and avoids the wide-interval extrapolation inherent in the regression-intercept approach; where the two corrections agree, the adjusted conclusion is robust to modelling choice.

These diagnostic results suggest that while mate-choice copying is a genuine ecological phenomenon, the overall raw pooled estimates from both continuous and binary literatures may be moderately inflated due to selective reporting and publication biases. Future research should prioritize large-sample pre-registered replication studies to provide unbiased baselines.

title: Cross-Dataset Extraction Validation
text:
What this document validates
We are updating a mate-choice-copying meta-analysis built on two existing datasets that overlap in the primary studies they cover but report different effect-size metrics:
Both are standardized effect sizes: different numerical scales for the same underlying quantity — how strongly an observer’s mate preference shifts toward the male it saw being chosen — and one scale can be converted into the other.
Before pooling them we need to know: for the studies both teams extracted, do the effect sizes agree once placed on a common scale? This is a method-comparison problem (two “instruments” measuring the same underlying effects), so — unlike a single-team re-extraction audit — we can apply formal agreement statistics (correlation, Bland–Altman limits of agreement) on top of the per-study qualitative review.
The matching itself (study-level crosswalk + within-study optimal effect-size assignment) is performed by crosscheck_davies_jones.py; this report reads its output and assesses agreement. Key methodological points carried over:
How this document is organised. The analysis runs in five steps: (1) match studies — find the studies both datasets extracted and pair up their individual effect sizes; (2) measure agreement — quantify how closely the paired values agree once on a common scale; (3) classify disagreements — for pairs that do not agree, label why; (4) re-extract the worst cases — return to the original PDFs and recompute the effect size from the raw data; and (5) conclude. Throughout, “Jones” and “Davies” name the two source datasets, not the primary studies they drew from. A pair is a single Jones effect size lined up with the one Davies effect size that most plausibly measures the same thing in the same study (the pairing is done automatically, choosing the alignment that minimises the magnitude difference within each study).
Show R code
# Resolve the outputs dir whether rendered standalone (cwd = this file's dir)
# or as part of the book (cwd = project root, via execute-dir: project).
out_dir <- "outputs/3_original_dataset_crosscheck"
if (!dir.exists(out_dir)) out_dir <- file.path("..", "..", out_dir)
xwalk  <- read_csv(file.path(out_dir, "crosswalk_effect_sizes.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
shared <- read_csv(file.path(out_dir, "shared_studies.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
unmatched <- read_csv(file.path(out_dir, "unmatched_jones_studies.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)

# study tag + agreement / structural-cause classification
xwalk <- xwalk %>%
    mutate(
        study      = paste0(author_prefix, year),
        agreement  = if_else(magnitude_match, "agree", "disagree"),
        es_structure = if_else(n_jones_es == n_davies_es,
                               "same ES count", "ES count differs"),
        cause = case_when(
            magnitude_match                      ~ "agree",
            n_jones_es != n_davies_es            ~ "ES-count / extraction structure differs",
            TRUE                                 ~ "same structure, magnitude conflict"
        )
    )

title: Cross-Dataset Extraction Validation
text:
Davies et al. (2020) — Hedges’ d
Jones & DuVal (2019) — log odds ratio (lnOR)





Scale conversion. Jones lnOR is mapped to Hedges’ d via the logistic-to-normal approximation \(d = \text{lnOR}\times\sqrt{3}/\pi\) (≈ 0.5513; Borenstein et al. 2009).
Absolute magnitudes. The two datasets use opposite sign conventions (Davies’ non-directional d carries the raw mean-difference sign; Jones’ lnOR sign encodes copying direction), so agreement is assessed on \(|d|\). Sign concordance is reported separately as a diagnostic.

title: Cross-Dataset Extraction Validation
text:
Headline summary


Show R code
n_shared <- nrow(shared)
n_pairs  <- nrow(xwalk)
n_match  <- sum(xwalk$magnitude_match)
n_sign   <- sum(xwalk$sign_agree)
tibble(
    Metric = c("Shared studies (in both datasets)",
               "Jones studies NOT in Davies",
               "Comparable effect-size pairs",
               "Pairs matching in magnitude (|Δd| ≤ 0.15 or ≤ 15%)",
               "Pairs with concordant sign"),
    Value  = c(n_shared,
               nrow(unmatched),
               n_pairs,
               sprintf("%d (%s%%)", n_match, fmt(100*n_match/n_pairs, 0)),
               sprintf("%d (%s%%)", n_sign,  fmt(100*n_sign/n_pairs, 0)))
) %>% kable()









Metric
Value




Shared studies (in both datasets)
29


Jones studies NOT in Davies
8


Comparable effect-size pairs
52


Pairs matching in magnitude (|Δd| ≤ 0.15 or ≤ 15%)
34 (65%)


Pairs with concordant sign
36 (69%)





How to read this. The two datasets share 29 primary studies, with a further 8 studies appearing only in Jones. Within the shared studies their effect sizes line up into 52 comparable pairs, of which 65% agree in magnitude (tolerance: 0.15 d-units or 15%). Sign agreement looks low (69%), but that is expected — the two datasets encode copying direction with opposite signs, so we compare absolute magnitudes here and revisit sign separately later. The sections that follow ask how serious the magnitude disagreements are, and what causes them.

title: Cross-Dataset Extraction Validation
text:
Method-comparison agreement statistics
With the effect sizes paired, we ask how well the two datasets agree as measurements of the same thing — the classic question of whether two instruments give the same reading. Three complementary tools answer it:

Correlation (Pearson r, Spearman ρ) — do the two rise and fall together? (1 = perfectly in step.)
Regression slope of Davies on Jones — is one dataset systematically larger than the other? (a slope of 1 = no scaling difference.)
Bland–Altman — for each pair, the difference between the two values plotted against their average; the mean difference is the typical bias, and the 95% “limits of agreement” bound how far apart the two datasets usually fall.



Show R code
pear <- cor.test(xwalk$abs_jones_d, xwalk$abs_davies_d, method = "pearson")
spear <- suppressWarnings(
    cor.test(xwalk$abs_jones_d, xwalk$abs_davies_d, method = "spearman"))
fit  <- lm(abs_davies_d ~ abs_jones_d, data = xwalk)

# same-ES-count subset (like-for-like extractions)
sub <- filter(xwalk, es_structure == "same ES count")
pear_sub <- cor.test(sub$abs_jones_d, sub$abs_davies_d)

# Bland-Altman on |d|
xwalk <- xwalk %>%
    mutate(ba_mean = (abs_jones_d + abs_davies_d)/2,
           ba_diff = abs_jones_d - abs_davies_d)
bias <- mean(xwalk$ba_diff); s <- sd(xwalk$ba_diff)
loa_lo <- bias - 1.96*s; loa_hi <- bias + 1.96*s

tibble(
    Statistic = c("Pearson r (all pairs)",
                  "Spearman ρ (all pairs)",
                  "Pearson r (same-ES-count studies only)",
                  "OLS slope (Davies ~ Jones)",
                  "OLS intercept",
                  "Bland–Altman mean bias (Jones − Davies, |d|)",
                  "95% limits of agreement"),
    Value = c(sprintf("%s (95%% CI %s–%s, %s)", fmt(pear$estimate,3),
                      fmt(pear$conf.int[1],2), fmt(pear$conf.int[2],2),
                      ifelse(pear$p.value < 1e-4, "p < 0.0001",
                             paste0("p = ", fmt(pear$p.value,4)))),
              fmt(spear$estimate,3),
              sprintf("%s (n = %d pairs)", fmt(pear_sub$estimate,3), nrow(sub)),
              fmt(coef(fit)[2],3),
              fmt(coef(fit)[1],3),
              sprintf("%s d-units", fmt(bias,3)),
              sprintf("%s to %s", fmt(loa_lo,2), fmt(loa_hi,2)))
) %>% kable()









Statistic
Value




Pearson r (all pairs)
0.795 (95% CI 0.67–0.88, p < 0.0001)


Spearman ρ (all pairs)
0.862


Pearson r (same-ES-count studies only)
0.891 (n = 12 pairs)


OLS slope (Davies ~ Jones)
1.193


OLS intercept
0.036


Bland–Altman mean bias (Jones − Davies, |d|)
-0.141 d-units


95% limits of agreement
-1.05 to 0.77





A negative mean bias (-0.14) and an OLS slope > 1 (1.19) both indicate Davies tends to report larger magnitudes than the Jones-converted values, increasingly so for larger effects.

Agreement plots
In the left panel, perfect agreement would place every point on the dashed diagonal; points above it are studies where Davies reports the larger magnitude. In the right (Bland–Altman) panel, points scattered evenly around zero would mean no systematic difference — a shift away from zero is bias, and points beyond the dashed limits are the outlying disagreements that the rest of this document investigates.


Show R code
lim <- c(0, max(xwalk$abs_jones_d, xwalk$abs_davies_d) * 1.05)

p_scatter <- ggplot(xwalk, aes(abs_jones_d, abs_davies_d)) +
    geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +
    geom_point(aes(colour = agreement, shape = es_structure), size = 2.6, alpha = .85) +
    geom_text(aes(label = study), size = 2.4, vjust = -0.8, check_overlap = TRUE) +
    scale_colour_manual(values = c(agree = "#2c7fb8", disagree = "#d95f0e")) +
    coord_equal(xlim = lim, ylim = lim) +
    labs(x = "Jones & DuVal  |d|  (lnOR × √3/π)", y = "Davies  |Hedges d|",
         colour = NULL, shape = NULL, title = "Effect-size agreement") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) + theme(legend.position = "bottom")

p_ba <- ggplot(xwalk, aes(ba_mean, ba_diff)) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, colour = "grey80") +
    geom_hline(yintercept = bias, colour = "#2c7fb8") +
    geom_hline(yintercept = c(loa_lo, loa_hi), linetype = "dashed", colour = "#d95f0e") +
    geom_point(aes(colour = agreement), size = 2.6, alpha = .85) +
    geom_text(aes(label = study), size = 2.4, vjust = -0.8, check_overlap = TRUE) +
    scale_colour_manual(values = c(agree = "#2c7fb8", disagree = "#d95f0e"), guide = "none") +
    labs(x = "Mean of the two |d|", y = "Jones − Davies  (|d|)",
         title = "Bland–Altman") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

p_scatter + p_ba








Figure 5.1: Left: Jones-converted vs Davies |d| with the line of identity (dashed). Right: Bland–Altman plot (mean bias solid, 95% limits of agreement dashed). Points are coloured by whether the pair matches in magnitude; studies where the two teams extracted different numbers of effect sizes are ringed.

title: Cross-Dataset Extraction Validation
text:
Discrepancy classification
Borrowing the per-study “required action” structure from Sánchez-Tójar et al.’s validation codebook, every pair is classified by why it does or does not agree. The dominant structural cause is whether the two teams extracted the same number of effect sizes from a paper.


Show R code
xwalk %>% count(cause, name = "pairs") %>%
    mutate(`%` = fmt(100*pairs/sum(pairs), 0)) %>% kable()





cause
pairs
%




ES-count / extraction structure differs
13
25


agree
34
65


same structure, magnitude conflict
5
10





The three causes mean very different things. Agree needs no action. ES-count / extraction structure differs means the two teams split a study into different numbers of effect sizes, so a clean one-to-one comparison is impossible — a bookkeeping difference, not necessarily an error. Same structure, magnitude conflict is the category that matters: the teams extracted the same number of effect sizes yet still disagree, which signals a genuine extraction difference. In the per-pair table below, the behaviour columns are the key tell — when a Jones “reversal/random” contrast is paired against a Davies “affiliation/mating” one, the two teams measured a different outcome from the same paper.


Show R code
xwalk %>%
    transmute(study, jones_code, jones_behaviour,
              davies_study, davies_behaviour, davies_n,
              `Jones |d|` = round(abs_jones_d, 2),
              `Davies |d|` = round(abs_davies_d, 2),
              `Δ|d|` = round(abs_diff, 2),
              sign_agree, cause) %>%
    datatable(rownames = FALSE, filter = "top",
              options = list(pageLength = 15, scrollX = TRUE),
              caption = "Per-pair crosswalk. Note the behaviour columns: a Jones 'reversal/random' contrast paired against a Davies 'Affiliation/Mating' contrast flags a different OUTCOME extracted from the same paper.")







Studies flagged for manual re-extraction
The clearest validation failures are pairs that disagree despite both teams extracting the same number of effect sizes (so the disagreement is not merely structural), plus any same-paper pair whose outcome construct differs.

Effect-size-count reconciliation
How the two teams partitioned each shared paper into effect sizes (a major driver of the comparable-pair count, since only min(nJ, nD) pairs per study can be aligned).

title: Cross-Dataset Extraction Validation
section: Re-extraction: what happens when we return to the source PDFs
text:
Re-extraction: what happens when we return to the source PDFs
The agreement analysis flags where the two datasets disagree, but not which is correct. To resolve that for the worst cases, we pulled the primary-study PDFs for every study whose effect-size pair disagreed by > 0.5 Hedges-d units and re-extracted each effect size from the raw data, recomputing it as a 2×2 odds ratio — tabulating how many observers chose the demonstrated male versus the alternative, in the treatment and (where one exists) in the control, and taking the odds of one over the other — under the meta-analysis’s own conventions (treatment-vs-control where the study used a control group; the observed proportion against 50% chance otherwise). This gives a third, independent estimate per effect size — a tie-breaker.


Show R code
reext <- read_csv(file.path(out_dir, "reextraction_flagged_7studies.csv"),
                  show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
    mutate(
        study     = gsub("_", " ", sub("^([^_]+_[0-9]+).*", "\\1", identifierStudyId)),
        label     = paste0(study, " · ", identifierExperimentId),
        reext_d   = log(effectSizeCalculatedEstimate) * 0.5513,   # ln(OR) -> Hedges d
        jones_d   = compare_jones_d,
        davies_d  = compare_davies_hedges_d,
        reext_abs = abs(reext_d), jones_abs = abs(jones_d), davies_abs = abs(davies_d)
    )
mj <- mean(abs(reext$reext_abs - reext$jones_abs))
md <- mean(abs(reext$reext_abs - reext$davies_abs))



The three estimates side by side


Show R code
reext %>%
    transmute(Study = label,
              `2×2 (A/B/C/D)` = paste(effectSizeSampleSizeA, effectSizeSampleSizeB,
                                      effectSizeSampleSizeC, effectSizeSampleSizeD, sep = "/"),
              `Re-extracted d` = round(reext_d, 2),
              `Jones d` = round(jones_d, 2),
              `Davies d` = round(davies_d, 2),
              `|re-ext − Jones|` = round(abs(reext_abs - jones_abs), 2),
              `|re-ext − Davies|` = round(abs(reext_abs - davies_abs), 2)) %>%
    datatable(rownames = FALSE, options = list(pageLength = 12, dom = "t", scrollX = TRUE),
              caption = "Re-extracted effect size vs the two prior extractions (Hedges d). Cells A/B = treatment chose-target / not; C/D = control or 50/50 reference.")






Across the 12 re-extracted effect sizes the point estimates sit almost exactly on the Jones values (mean |Δd| = 0.00) and far from the Davies values (mean |Δd| = 0.83). Two cautions stop this from being read as “the extraction is validated against Jones”:

Point-estimate agreement is expected, not corroborating. These outcomes are discrete choice counts read directly from each PDF; any careful extractor building the same 2×2 obtains the same lnOR. Agreement here confirms we read the papers the same way — it is not independent evidence that either prior extraction is correct.
The original crosswalk selected the construction that matched Jones. For each study it chose the 2×2 that reproduced Jones’s variance, which makes the variance match circular. The next section removes that circularity: it re-derives every effect size from a rule fixed by the study design before any comparison to Jones, and reports count agreement and design agreement separately.



Visualising the tie-break


Show R code
ord  <- reext %>% arrange(reext_abs) %>% pull(label)
long <- reext %>%
    select(label, Jones = jones_abs, Davies = davies_abs, `Re-extracted` = reext_abs) %>%
    pivot_longer(-label, names_to = "source", values_to = "absd") %>%
    mutate(label = factor(label, levels = ord))

p_db <- ggplot(long, aes(absd, label)) +
    geom_line(aes(group = label), colour = "grey75") +
    geom_point(aes(colour = source, shape = source), size = 3, alpha = .9) +
    scale_colour_manual(values = c(`Re-extracted` = "#1a9850", Jones = "#2c7fb8", Davies = "#d95f0e")) +
    labs(x = "|Hedges d|", y = NULL, colour = NULL, shape = NULL,
         title = "Re-extracted vs prior extractions") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 11) + theme(legend.position = "bottom")

dev <- reext %>%
    transmute(label = factor(label, levels = ord),
              `vs Jones`  = abs(reext_abs - jones_abs),
              `vs Davies` = abs(reext_abs - davies_abs)) %>%
    pivot_longer(-label, names_to = "comparison", values_to = "dev")
p_dev <- ggplot(dev, aes(dev, label, fill = comparison)) +
    geom_col(position = "dodge") +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c(`vs Jones` = "#2c7fb8", `vs Davies` = "#d95f0e")) +
    labs(x = "|re-extracted − prior|  (Hedges d)", y = NULL, fill = NULL,
         title = "Deviation from re-extraction") +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
    theme(legend.position = "bottom", axis.text.y = element_blank())
p_db + p_dev








Figure 5.2: Left: each flagged effect size on the |Hedges d| axis — re-extracted (independent, from the PDF) vs Jones vs Davies; the re-extracted point sits on Jones in every case, with Davies displaced. Right: absolute deviation of each prior extraction from the re-extracted value.







Count agreement vs design agreement (and a correction to Jones)
To break the circularity we re-derive each effect size from a rule fixed by the study design alone — before looking at Jones — and report two things separately: count agreement (does our lnOR from the raw counts match Jones, i.e. did we read the paper the same way) and design agreement (does our variance and N match, i.e. did we model the design the same way). The rule has two branches:

Real control group — the 2×2 has distinct treatment and control cells (e.g. Dugatkin 1992’s naïve non-observer group). A standard 2×2 odds ratio is valid: \(\mathrm{Var}=1/A+1/B+1/C+1/D\), \(N=A+B+C+D\).
Single group vs 50 % chance — the “50/50 rule” sets \(C=D=n/2\). The design-faithful estimator is the one-sample logit of the observed proportion: \(\mathrm{Var}=1/A+1/B\), \(N=A+B\). The 50/50 group is a phantom control: it invents \(n\) observations that were never collected and inflates the variance by exactly \(4/n\) while doubling the reported \(N\).



Show R code
reext2 <- reext %>%
    mutate(
        A = effectSizeSampleSizeA, B = effectSizeSampleSizeB,
        C = effectSizeSampleSizeC, D = effectSizeSampleSizeD,
        design = if_else(C == D, "single group vs 50% chance", "real control group"),
        corr_var = if_else(design == "real control group",
                           1/A + 1/B + 1/C + 1/D, 1/A + 1/B),
        corr_N   = if_else(design == "real control group", A + B + C + D, A + B),
        jones_var    = compare_jones_VlnOR,
        count_agree  = abs(reext_d - jones_d) < 0.02,   # point estimate (lnOR × √3/π)
        design_agree = abs(corr_var - jones_var) < 0.01
    )

reext2 %>% summarise(
    `Effect sizes` = n(),
    `Count agreement (lnOR matches Jones)`      = sum(count_agree),
    `Design agreement (variance matches Jones)` = sum(design_agree)
) %>% pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Quantity", values_to = "n") %>%
    kable()





Quantity
n




Effect sizes
12


Count agreement (lnOR matches Jones)
12


Design agreement (variance matches Jones)
7





Count agreement is complete; design agreement holds only for the real-control studies, where a 2×2 is the correct model. The single-group studies disagree by construction — and the disagreement is Jones’s \(4/n\) phantom-control inflation:


Show R code
reext2 %>%
    filter(design == "single group vs 50% chance") %>%
    transmute(Study = label,
              `our Var (1/A+1/B)` = round(corr_var, 4),
              `+ 4/n`             = round(4/(A + B), 4),
              `our Var + 4/n`     = round(corr_var + 4/(A + B), 4),
              `Jones Var`         = round(jones_var, 4),
              `our N` = corr_N,
              Note = if_else(abs(corr_var + 4/(A + B) - jones_var) < 0.01,
                             "Jones inflated by phantom 50/50 control",
                             "Jones used a different null")) %>%
    kable()
